overview.title: Constitution of the United States of America
overview.subtitle: A modern replacement draft built around democratic legitimacy, anti-authoritarian safeguards, and readable constitutional architecture.
overview.top_strength: The architecture is now materially cleaner and the simulator still shows a closure-oriented system with 0 unresolved obligations even under a larger, harsher suite; recent validation and compression passes have shifted the project firmly into final-refinement territory
overview.top_weakness: The remaining weakness is no longer missing machinery; it is a mix of deadline-driven political nonperformance, a few judicial and institutional delay chains, and residual code-like density in a handful of articles

docs.title: Preamble
docs.summary: We the People of the United States, all of us without exception, establish this Constitution.
docs.search_text: Preamble Status: [ DRAFT ] The Preamble We the People of the United States, all of us without exception, establish this Constitution. Our nation was founded on the promise that all are created equal and possess rights no government may rightfully deny. For too long, that promise was honored for some and denied to others. This Constitution is our covenant to make it real for all. We establish a government that serves the people and remains answerable to them: strong enough to protect, limited enough to remain free, and bound by law in every exercise of public power. We know that unchecked power corrodes, that rights without remedies are promises without force, and that democracy, if left undefended, may be captured by those who claim its authority while defying its laws and equal citizenship. We act not for ourselves alone. We honor those who came before us and were denied the equal protection of this promise, and we accept a duty to those who will come after us. This Constitution binds us to leave them a republic capable of self government, a nation capable of justice, and a planet capable of sustaining life. To these ends, we ordain and establish this Constitution of the United States of America.

docs.title: Article I — The Electoral System
docs.summary: Establishes the right to vote, the mechanics of all federal elections, the rules for political party participation, and the citizens' initiative process. Election administration is delegated to the Electoral Commission under Article XII.

docs.search_text: Article I — The Electoral System Status: [ DRAFT ] Scope Establishes the right to vote, the mechanics of all federal elections, the rules for political party participation, and the citizens' initiative process. Election administration is delegated to the Electoral Commission under Article XII. Section 1 — The Right to Vote 1.1 Every citizen of the United States who is eighteen years of age or older has the right to vote in all federal elections. This right shall not be denied, abridged, or conditioned on any basis including race, color, sex, gender, sexual orientation, religion, national origin, disability, income, property, or prior criminal conviction for which a sentence has been completed. 1.2 The right to vote is a fundamental constitutional right. Any law, regulation, order, or practice that burdens the exercise of this right is subject to strict scrutiny by the federal courts. 1.3 Every citizen entitled to vote has an equal right to have their vote counted. No electoral system, district boundary, or administrative procedure may be designed or administered with the purpose or effect of diluting the voting power of any group of citizens. 1.4 Voting in federal elections is a right, not an obligation. No penalty may be imposed for failure to vote. Section 2 — Voter Registration 2.1 Every citizen is automatically registered to vote upon turning eighteen years of age, or upon becoming a citizen, using information held by federal and state agencies. No citizen is required to take affirmative action to register. For persons naturalizing as adults, registration shall be completed through the naturalization process itself — the Electoral Commission shall coordinate with the agency responsible for administering naturalization to ensure that every new citizen is registered in their state of domicile at the time their naturalization is certified, without any separate application required. 2.2 Citizens may update their registration at any time, including on the day of an election. 2.3 A citizen's registration may be removed only upon their death, upon their written request, or upon a judicial finding of ineligibility. Removal on any other basis is unlawful. 2.4 The Electoral Commission (Article XII) shall maintain the national voter registration system and publish the full register, redacted for personal security information, as a public record. Section 3 — Federal Election Schedule 3.1 Federal elections shall be held on the first Tuesday of November in even numbered years. This schedule may not be altered by executive order or declaration of emergency. Congress may by law move the election date only in the event of a catastrophic national emergency, and only by a 2/3 vote of both chambers, and only to a date within 60 days of the original date. 3.2 All federal elections shall be conducted over a period of no less than three days, ending on the Tuesday specified in Section 3.1. Early voting shall be available for no less than fifteen days before the election. For purposes of Section 3.1, a "catastrophic national emergency" that may justify congressional action to move the election date is limited to: (i) armed conflict occurring on United States soil; (ii) a pandemic causing the death of at least 10,000 persons per week nationally as certified by the Director of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention; or (iii) a natural disaster that has destroyed critical infrastructure in three or more states as certified by the Federal Emergency Management Agency. No single criterion is independently sufficient — an electoral delay requires a concurrent affirmative finding by all three branches: a presidential declaration, a 2/3 vote of both chambers of Congress, and a written determination by a majority of the Supreme Court that the emergency renders the conduct of a free and fair election on the scheduled date impossible. 3.3 Election Day shall be a federal public holiday. 3.4 No federal official may declare a national emergency within 60 days of a scheduled federal election in a manner that restricts access to polling places, reduces early voting, or delays certification of results, unless a federal court finds the emergency poses a direct, imminent, and unavoidable threat to public safety that cannot be mitigated by other means. Any such court proceeding shall be subject to strict scrutiny. Standing to challenge an emergency declaration under this Section is held by any registered voter and by the Electoral Commission. Courts shall resolve such challenges on an expedited basis, with a final ruling issued within 72 hours of the filing of a complaint. Section 4 — Elections to the House of Representatives 4.1 — Method: Single Transferable Vote Elections to the House of Representatives shall use the Single Transferable Vote method. Voters shall rank candidates in order of preference. Seats shall be allocated by the Droop quota formula. Voters shall be permitted to rank a number of candidates not less than the number of seats in their district. The Electoral Commission shall set the maximum number of rankings by regulation, calibrated to ballot usability. A ballot that ranks fewer than the maximum number of candidates remains valid and shall not be treated as exhausted until all ranked candidates have been eliminated or elected. 4.2 — Multi Member Districts The nation shall be divided into congressional districts. Each district shall return between three and seven members to the House of Representatives. The number of members returned by each district shall be determined by the Independent Redistricting Commission (Section 8) in proportion to the district's population, subject to the three member minimum. 4.3 — Terms Members of the House of Representatives shall serve four year terms. Districts shall be divided by the Independent Redistricting Commission into two equal groups. Group A districts shall hold elections in years divisible by four; Group B districts shall hold elections in years producing a remainder of two when divided by four. This staggering ensures that approximately half of all House seats are contested every two years. 4.4 — Vacancies A vacancy in the House of Representatives shall be filled by a special election held within 90 days of the vacancy arising. If fewer than 6 months remain in the term, the seat may remain vacant until the next scheduled general election. The Electoral Commission shall complete certification of any special election within 45 days of the election being held, regardless of any pending disputes. If the Commission fails to certify within that period, certification shall proceed automatically under court supervision, with the United States District Court for the District of Columbia designated to appoint a special master to complete certification within 15 additional days. Section 5 — Elections to the Regional Assembly 5.1 — Method: Ranked Choice Voting Elections to the Regional Assembly shall use Ranked Choice Voting (Instant Runoff). Each state shall elect its Regional Assembly members in statewide elections. Voters shall rank candidates in order of preference. The candidate with the fewest first preference votes is eliminated in each round and their votes redistributed until a candidate receives a majority. 5.2 — Staggered Terms Members of the Regional Assembly shall serve six year terms. Regional Assembly seats within each region shall be divided into three equal classes. One class within each region shall face election every two years, ensuring continuity of representation. 5.3 — Vacancies A vacancy in the Regional Assembly shall be filled by special election held within 120 days of the vacancy arising. If fewer than 12 months remain in the term, the Governor of the relevant state shall appoint a temporary replacement who shall serve until the next scheduled general election, at which point a special election shall fill the remainder of the term. The Electoral Commission shall complete certification of any special election within 45 days of the election being held, regardless of any pending disputes. If the Commission fails to certify within that period, certification shall proceed automatically under court supervision, with the United States District Court for the District of Columbia designated to appoint a special master to complete certification within 15 additional days. Section 6 — Presidential Election 6.1 The President shall be elected by direct national popular vote using Ranked Choice Voting. The candidate who achieves a majority of votes after the elimination of lower ranked candidates shall be elected President. 6.2 The Electoral College is abolished. No intermediate body shall stand between the national popular vote and the election of the President. 6.3 The Electoral Commission shall certify the result of the presidential election no later than 30 days after Election Day. Certification may be delayed only by order of a federal court with jurisdiction over an active election dispute. Litigation filed primarily to delay certification rather than to vindicate a genuine legal claim is frivolous and subjects the filing party and their counsel to sanctions under the applicable rules of federal civil procedure and any additional sanctions established by the Electoral Commission by regulation. If no federal court has issued a stay of certification by day 30, certification proceeds automatically without further action by any officer or body. 6.4 Presidential and Vice Presidential candidates shall run jointly on a single ticket. The Vice President is elected as part of the same ticket as the President and has no separate electoral mandate. Section 7 — Apportionment 7.1 A census of the population shall be conducted every ten years, in years ending in zero, by an agency independent of partisan direction. The census shall count all persons residing in the United States regardless of citizenship status. 7.2 Following each census, the 500 seats of the House of Representatives shall be reapportioned among the states in proportion to their populations, with each state guaranteed a minimum of one seat. 7.3 Following each census, Regional Assembly seats within each region shall be reapportioned among the states of that region in proportion to their populations, with each state guaranteed a minimum of one seat in the Regional Assembly. 7.4 Reapportionment shall take effect for the first election held more than one year after the census is completed. Section 8 — Independent Redistricting 8.1 Congressional district boundaries for House elections shall be drawn by the Independent Redistricting Commission, an independent body within the Electoral Commission (Article XII). 8.2 Districts shall be drawn according to the following criteria, in order of priority: (a) Equal population, within a deviation of no more than 1% (b) Contiguity — all parts of a district must be connected (c) Compliance with the Voting Rights provisions of Article V (d) Preservation of existing communities of interest, including municipal and county boundaries where possible (e) Geographic compactness (f) Partisan symmetry — the map as a whole shall not produce a systematic electoral advantage for any political party disproportionate to that party's statewide vote share. The Electoral Commission shall establish by regulation the methods and metrics used to measure partisan symmetry, which shall include at minimum the efficiency gap and mean median difference. A map that produces a projected partisan bias exceeding a threshold established by the Commission shall not be adopted without written findings explaining why no compliant alternative exists. This Constitution establishes the following floors that the Commission may tighten but may not loosen: the efficiency gap for any adopted map may not exceed 7%; the mean median difference may not exceed 5%. These are maximum permissible values — the Commission retains authority to adopt stricter standards by regulation. (g) No criterion may consider the political affiliation, voting history, or place of residence of any individual voter for the purpose of favoring or disfavoring any party. Partisan symmetry under Section 8.2(f) is measured as an output constraint on the map — it does not permit drawing lines with partisan intent. 8.3 District maps shall be drawn using open, publicly auditable algorithmic methods. All data, algorithms, and intermediate outputs used in drawing maps shall be published before the maps take effect. 8.4 Proposed maps shall be subject to a 60 day public comment period. The Commission shall publish a written response to all substantive comments received. 8.5 Final maps may be challenged in federal court within 30 days of adoption. Courts shall apply the criteria of Section 8.2 in reviewing any challenge. A court finding a map non compliant shall order the Commission to produce a remedial map within 60 days. 8.6 — Deadlock resolution. If the Commission cannot adopt a final map by majority vote within 30 days of the close of the public comment period, or cannot adopt a court ordered remedial map within the required period, the following process applies: (a) Each faction of the Commission that has submitted a competing map proposal shall certify its proposal to the Chief Judge of the United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit within 5 days (b) The Chief Judge shall convene a panel of three federal circuit judges, selected at random from circuits other than the D.C. Circuit, to serve as the redistricting panel (c) The panel shall select one of the submitted proposals — without modification — within 30 days, applying the criteria of Section 8.2 as its sole standard (d) The panel may not draw its own map; it may only select among the proposals submitted by the Commission (e) The panel's selection is final and not subject to appeal except on the grounds that the selected map violates the criteria of Section 8.2 (f) If all proposals submitted to the panel fail to meet the constitutional criteria of Section 8.2, the panel shall not select a non compliant map without first issuing written notice to each Commission faction identifying the deficiencies and requesting revised proposals within 15 days. If the revised proposals remain non compliant, the panel shall select the least non compliant proposal and shall simultaneously certify the map to the United States District Court for the District of Columbia for immediate judicial review. The selected map takes provisional effect pending that review, but the court shall issue a final ruling within 60 days and may order further remediation. Section 9 — Party Qualification and Representation Threshold 9.1 Any political party that obtains signatures of at least 0.1% of registered voters in at least 20 states within a 12 month period shall be a qualified party entitled to appear on federal election ballots. 9.2 A party that wins at least 4% of the total national vote cast in elections to the House of Representatives shall be entitled to have its candidates considered for seat allocation under the STV formula in those elections. For purposes of this threshold, parties that are legally distinct but functionally unified shall be aggregated: two or more parties whose votes would otherwise be counted separately shall be treated as a single party if, in the most recent comparable federal election cycle, they shared a common organizational structure, shared top officers (including the same chairperson or executive director), or ran the same individual as a candidate in more than 30% of contested House districts. The Electoral Commission shall make aggregation determinations before each election, with findings subject to expedited judicial review. 9.3 The threshold in Section 9.2 does not apply to individual candidates running without party affiliation. Independent candidates may win seats through the STV process regardless of national vote totals. 9.4 No law may prohibit the formation or operation of a political party on the basis of its ideology, platform, or political positions, except where the party's stated purpose is the violent overthrow of this Constitution or the permanent removal of the right to vote. 9.5 A qualified party, and any independent or party candidate lawfully entitled to appear on a federal ballot, is entitled under neutral and publicly stated rules to nondiscriminatory access to core public or publicly regulated election infrastructure necessary for meaningful federal contestation. Congress may by law authorize the Electoral Commission to define and enforce such access with respect to official ballot information systems, publicly funded or officially designated candidate debates, public voter information feeds, and other election related channels whose exclusionary control would materially degrade effective political competition. This Section does not require private persons to endorse, sponsor, or carry partisan viewpoints except where Article VI permits narrowly tailored continuity or nondiscrimination duties for dominant civic intermediaries. Section 10 — Election Administration 10.1 All federal elections shall be administered by the Electoral Commission established under Article XII. No federal or state official subject to election may direct, supervise, or exercise authority over the administration of any election in which they are a candidate. 10.2 The Electoral Commission shall establish uniform minimum standards for federal elections, including ballot design, voting equipment, accessibility for voters with disabilities, and procedures for counting and certifying results. States may exceed but not fall below these standards. 10.3 All voting systems used in federal elections must produce a paper record verifiable by the voter at the time of voting. No voting system that does not produce such a record may be used in a federal election. 10.4 Results shall be certified by the Electoral Commission within 30 days of Election Day. Certification may be delayed only by order of a federal court. No elected or appointed official may refuse to certify, delay certification without court order, or substitute their judgment for the certified result. 10.5 Attempts to interfere with the administration of an election, intimidate voters or election workers, or obstruct the counting or certification of votes are federal crimes and constitute grounds for the anti subversion penalties of Article VI. 10.6 The Electoral Commission may issue expedited temporary orders, subject to prompt judicial review, to prevent or remedy discriminatory exclusion of qualified parties or lawful candidates from the election infrastructure described in Section 9.5 where delay would materially impair a pending federal election. Section 11 — Citizens' Initiative 11.1 Citizens may initiate a national referendum on the repeal or affirmation of any existing federal law by submitting a petition signed by at least 3% of registered voters, with signatures gathered from at least 30 states. 11.2 A petition meeting the requirements of Section 11.1 shall trigger a national referendum administered by the Electoral Commission, held at the next scheduled federal election or within 12 months, whichever is sooner. 11.3 A referendum under this Section passes upon approval by a majority of votes cast, provided that at least 40% of registered voters participate. Deliberate coordinated abstention organized for the purpose of defeating the participation floor — rather than genuine non participation — is itself a violation of democratic integrity. The Electoral Commission may investigate credible evidence of coordinated abstention campaigns and, upon a finding of probable cause, refer the matter to the Accountability Commission for appropriate action. Evidence sufficient for a referral includes public statements, organizational communications, or patterns of conduct demonstrating that the abstention was organized as a strategic tactic to cause the referendum to fail the participation threshold rather than to express genuine non participation. 11.4 A law repealed by referendum may not be re enacted in substantially the same form for four years without another referendum. 11.5 The Citizens' Initiative may not be used to: (a) Reduce, restrict, or repeal any right enumerated in Article V (b) Target any specific individual, group, or minority for differential treatment (c) Override a judicial ruling on constitutional grounds (d) Amend this Constitution (which is governed by Article XI) 11.6 The Supreme Court shall have jurisdiction to review any proposed referendum for compliance with Section 11.5 before it is put to a vote. Section 12 — Overseas Citizens 12.1 — Constitutional basis. The constitutional principles governing overseas citizenship, domicile assignment, and continued federal political membership are established in Article IX. This Section governs the administration of overseas federal voting. 12.2 — Electoral assignment. Congress and the Electoral Commission shall assign overseas citizens to the proper federal electoral home consistent with Article IX and shall prevent duplicate registration or multiple state participation in the same election. 12.3 — Registration. Overseas citizens shall register with the Electoral Commission through a secure federal overseas voter registration system. The Electoral Commission shall administer overseas balloting, maintain overseas registration rolls, and publish guidance sufficient to make overseas federal voting practical and reviewable. 12.4 — Ballot delivery and return. The Electoral Commission shall provide reliable ballot delivery and ballot return methods, including electronic and postal options where lawful and secure, sufficient to allow overseas citizens to cast timely ballots in federal elections. 12.5 — Counting rule. Overseas ballots received within 30 days after election day shall be counted, provided the ballot was cast on or before election day. Section 13 — Recall of Elected Federal Officials 13.1 — Purpose. Impeachment and chamber expulsion are the primary mechanisms for removing federal officials for misconduct. This Section provides a separate, constituent initiated recall mechanism for elected members of Congress and the Regional Assembly where those mechanisms have failed or where the ground for removal is loss of constituent confidence rather than formal misconduct. The presidential recall is governed by Article III, Section 14. 13.2 — Scope. The recall mechanism established in this Section applies to: (a) Members of the House of Representatives, recallable by the registered voters of their district (b) Members of the Regional Assembly, recallable by the registered voters of their state Federal judges and appointed officials are not subject to recall. Judicial independence requires insulation from popular pressure; the remedy for judicial misconduct is the process established in Article IV. 13.3 — Petition requirements. A recall election is triggered when a petition is submitted to the Electoral Commission meeting all of the following requirements: (a) Signatures : The petition must be signed by registered voters equal to at least 20% of the total votes cast in the official's most recent election, gathered from within the official's district or state (b) Collection period : Signatures must be gathered within 120 consecutive days (c) Verification : The Electoral Commission shall verify signatures within 30 days of submission and certify whether the threshold has been met (d) Timing restriction : A petition may not be filed during the first 12 months of the official's term, or within 6 months of the next scheduled general election for that seat (e) One attempt per term : Only one recall petition against the same official may be filed in any single term of office. A petition that fails to reach the signature threshold, or that results in a recall election in which the official survives, may not be refiled during the same term. 13.4 — Recall election. Upon certification of a valid petition, the Electoral Commission shall conduct a recall election within 60 days. (a) The ballot shall present a single question: whether the official should be removed from office (b) Removal requires a majority of votes cast, provided that total turnout equals at least 40% of registered voters in the district or state. If the turnout threshold is not met, the recall fails regardless of the vote distribution. Deliberate coordinated abstention organized for the purpose of defeating the participation floor is itself a violation of democratic integrity; the Electoral Commission may investigate and refer credible evidence of such conduct to the Accountability Commission, applying the same standard as Section 11.3 (c) The official subject to recall may campaign against removal and shall not be suspended from their duties pending the outcome 13.5 — Vacancy following recall. If an official is removed by recall: (a) For a House member : a special election shall be held in the district within 90 days, conducted by Single Transferable Vote among newly nominated candidates. The original STV count from the prior general election is not re used. The winner of the special election serves the remainder of the recalled member's original term, preserving the district's stagger class. A special election does not reset the term clock or alter the district's scheduled general election date. (b) For a Regional Assembly member : a special election shall be held statewide within 90 days, conducted by Ranked Choice Voting among newly nominated candidates (c) If fewer than 6 months remain in the term at the time of removal, the seat shall remain vacant until the next scheduled general election 13.6 — No recall for protected conduct. A recall petition may not cite as its basis any vote cast, speech made, or position taken by the official in the exercise of their legislative duties. The recall mechanism is a check on the official's conduct and fitness for office, not a mechanism for overriding legislative votes the petitioners disagree with. The Electoral Commission shall reject any petition whose stated basis primarily consists of the official's legislative record. Where a petition combines protected legislative conduct with non legislative allegations, the Electoral Commission may look through pretextual non legislative allegations — that is, allegations included solely to avoid the bar rather than reflecting a genuine independent ground — and shall reject the petition if, stripping away the pretextual allegations, the dominant and operative basis remains protected legislative conduct. 13.7 — Administration. The Electoral Commission shall administer all recall petitions and recall elections under this Section. It shall publish regulations governing petition form, signature verification, and recall election procedures.

docs.title: Article II — The Legislature
docs.summary: Establishes the structure, composition, powers, and procedures of the federal legislature. The legislature consists of two chambers: the House of Representatives and the Regional Assembly. Together they form the Congress of the United States.

docs.search_text: Article II — The Legislature Status: [ DRAFT ] Scope Establishes the structure, composition, powers, and procedures of the federal legislature. The legislature consists of two chambers: the House of Representatives and the Regional Assembly. Together they form the Congress of the United States. Section 1 — The Congress 1.1 All federal legislative power is vested in the Congress of the United States, which shall consist of the House of Representatives and the Regional Assembly. 1.2 The Congress shall meet in regular session beginning on the third day of January each year. Neither chamber may adjourn for more than three days without the consent of the other, nor may either chamber adjourn to a location outside the seat of government without the consent of both chambers and the President. 1.3 The proceedings of both chambers shall be recorded and published in full. No session of either chamber may be closed to the public record except by a 2/3 vote of the chamber for reasons of national security, and the fact of the closed session shall itself be published. Section 2 — The House of Representatives 2.1 Composition. The House of Representatives shall consist of 500 members elected from congressional districts as established under Article I. 2.2 Terms. Members of the House shall serve four year terms, with districts staggered into two groups as provided in Article I, Section 4.3. 2.3 Qualifications. A member of the House must be: (a) At least 21 years of age at the time of taking office (b) A citizen of the United States for at least five years (c) A resident of the state from which they are elected at the time of election 2.4 Speaker. The House shall elect a Speaker from among its members at the beginning of each new Congress. The Speaker shall preside over the House, set the legislative calendar, and represent the House in relations with the other branches. The Speaker may be removed by a majority vote of the House at any time. 2.5 Officers. The House shall elect such other officers as it deems necessary. No officer of the House may simultaneously hold any other federal office. Section 3 — The Regional Assembly 3.1 Composition. The Regional Assembly shall consist of approximately 100 members elected from the states within each geographic region, as defined in Section 4. The precise number of seats shall be reapportioned following each census as provided in Article I, Section 7. 3.2 Terms. Members of the Regional Assembly shall serve six year terms. Seats within each region shall be divided into three equal classes rotating on a two year election cycle, as provided in Article I, Section 5.2. 3.3 Qualifications. A member of the Regional Assembly must be: (a) At least 25 years of age at the time of taking office (b) A citizen of the United States for at least seven years (c) A resident of the state from which they are elected at the time of election 3.4 President of the Regional Assembly. The Vice President of the United States shall preside over the Regional Assembly but shall have no vote except to break a tie. The Regional Assembly shall also elect a President Pro Tempore from among its members to preside in the Vice President's absence. 3.5 Officers. The Regional Assembly shall elect such other officers as it deems necessary. Section 4 — Geographic Regions The states of the United States are organized into the following eight geographic regions for purposes of Regional Assembly apportionment. Regional boundaries may be altered only by constitutional amendment under Article XI, Track 1. | Region | States | | | | | Northeast | Maine, Vermont, New Hampshire, Massachusetts, Rhode Island, Connecticut, New York, New Jersey | | Mid Atlantic | Pennsylvania, Delaware, Maryland, Virginia, West Virginia | | Southeast | North Carolina, South Carolina, Georgia, Florida | | South | Alabama, Mississippi, Tennessee, Kentucky, Arkansas, Louisiana | | Midwest | Ohio, Indiana, Michigan, Wisconsin, Illinois, Minnesota, Iowa, Missouri | | Great Plains | North Dakota, South Dakota, Nebraska, Kansas, Oklahoma, Texas | | Mountain West | Montana, Idaho, Wyoming, Colorado, Utah, Nevada, Arizona, New Mexico | | Pacific | Washington, Oregon, California, Alaska, Hawaii | 4.1 Washington D.C. shall be treated as a state for purposes of Regional Assembly representation and shall be assigned to the Mid Atlantic region. 4.2 Any territory admitted as a state shall be assigned to the most geographically appropriate region by act of Congress at the time of admission. 4.3 Seats within each region shall be allocated among the states of that region in proportion to their populations following each census, with a minimum of one seat per state. 4.4 Each region shall be guaranteed a minimum of eight seats in the Regional Assembly regardless of population, to ensure regional coherence. Seats above this floor shall be allocated among all regions in proportion to national population. Section 5 — Disqualification and Removal 5.1 No person shall serve in Congress who has been convicted of a felony involving corruption, fraud, or abuse of public office, unless pardoned in accordance with Article III. 5.2 No person shall simultaneously serve as a member of Congress and hold any other federal office, state office, or position of profit under the United States. 5.3 A member of Congress may be expelled by a 2/3 vote of their chamber. Grounds for expulsion include conviction of a felony, a finding of serious ethics violation by the Congressional Ethics Commission, or conduct that the chamber determines to be incompatible with the duties of office. 5.3A Members of the House of Representatives and the Regional Assembly are subject to constituent initiated recall under Article I, Section 13. Recall is a separate mechanism from expulsion and does not require a finding of misconduct — it reflects loss of constituent confidence. The procedures, thresholds, and restrictions governing recall are set out in Article I, Section 13. 5.4 A member under active criminal indictment for a felony involving their official duties shall be suspended from voting in their chamber pending the outcome of proceedings, but shall not lose their seat until conviction. 5.5 — Felony conviction and removal: two categories. (a) Category 1 — Corruption and abuse of office: automatic removal. A member of Congress who is finally convicted of any of the following offenses shall be automatically removed from office upon the exhaustion of all appeals or the expiration of the time to appeal, whichever is earlier: bribery; extortion; fraud committed in an official capacity; self dealing or misuse of public funds; perjury or obstruction of justice in an official proceeding; abuse of official power; electoral subversion under Article VI; or any conspiracy to commit any of the foregoing. These offenses directly negate the trust placed in the office and admit no exception based on motivation or context. (b) Category 2 — All other felony convictions: chamber vote required. A member convicted of any other felony does not lose their seat automatically. The relevant chamber shall vote within 60 days of final conviction on whether to expel the member under Section 5.3. In making this determination the chamber may consider all relevant circumstances, including whether the conduct constituted principled civil disobedience, whether the prosecution appears to have been politically motivated, and whether the conduct bears on the member's fitness to serve. Expulsion under this subsection requires a 2/3 vote. (c) Appeal stay. For Category 2 offenses, removal does not take effect while a conviction is under direct appeal. A member subject to a Category 2 conviction may continue to serve pending the outcome of their appeal. If the conviction is upheld on final appeal, the chamber vote under Section 5.5(b) shall be held within 60 days of that final determination. (d) Reinstatement. If a member's conviction is overturned on appeal after they have been removed under this Section, they may stand for election at the next available opportunity but are not automatically reinstated to their seat. Section 6 — Compensation and Conflicts 6.1 Members of Congress shall receive compensation set by law. No law increasing congressional compensation shall take effect until after an election of the House of Representatives has intervened. 6.2 Members of Congress shall file complete financial disclosures as required by Article VIII and by the Accountability Commission (Article XII). 6.3 No member of Congress may hold a financial interest in any matter before their chamber, vote on any legislation from which they stand to derive direct personal financial benefit, or accept gifts, payments, or compensation from any person or entity seeking to influence legislation, beyond the compensation established by law. Section 7 — Exclusive Powers of the House The House of Representatives alone shall have the power to: (a) Originate revenue and appropriations legislation. All bills raising revenue or appropriating federal funds must originate in the House. The Regional Assembly may propose amendments to such bills but may not originate them. (b) Initiate impeachment. The House alone may bring articles of impeachment against the President, Vice President, federal judges, members of the Regional Assembly, and other federal officers. A simple majority of the House is required to approve articles of impeachment. (c) Elect the President in a contingent election. If no presidential candidate achieves a majority under ranked choice voting, the House shall elect the President from the top two candidates by vote of the full membership, with each member casting one vote. Section 8 — Exclusive Powers of the Regional Assembly The Regional Assembly alone shall have the power to: (a) Try impeachments. When the House has approved articles of impeachment, the Regional Assembly shall conduct the trial. Conviction requires a 2/3 vote. Upon conviction, the officer is removed from office and may be disqualified from future office by a subsequent majority vote. Criminal prosecution may follow separately. (b) Confirm appointments. The Regional Assembly shall confirm or reject presidential nominations for: Supreme Court justices, federal appellate judges, Cabinet members, ambassadors, the heads of independent agencies, and members of the Constitutional Organs (Article XII). Confirmation requires a majority vote. A nomination not acted upon within 90 days of submission is deemed confirmed. (c) Ratify treaties. The Regional Assembly shall ratify treaties negotiated by the President. Ratification requires a 2/3 vote. An executive agreement that would, if enacted as law, require legislation must be submitted as a treaty. (d) Confirm declarations of national emergency. A declaration of national emergency by the President must be approved by the Regional Assembly within 30 days or it lapses, as provided in Article III. Section 9 — Joint Powers of Congress Both chambers, acting together, shall have the power to: (a) Declare war. A declaration of war requires a majority vote of both chambers in joint session. No declaration of war may be issued against a nation that has not attacked the United States or a treaty ally, except by a 2/3 vote of both chambers. (b) Override a presidential veto. A bill returned by the President with objections may be enacted over the veto by a 2/3 vote of both chambers. (c) Propose constitutional amendments. As provided in Article XI. (d) Authorize all other federal powers enumerated in this Constitution, including taxation, borrowing, regulation of commerce, establishment of federal courts below the Supreme Court, and all laws necessary and proper to carry out these powers. 9.1 — Enumerated Powers. Congress may exercise only the powers granted to it by this Constitution. The enumeration of specific powers shall not be construed to deny or disparage powers reasonably implied by those expressly granted, but Congress may not use implied powers to circumvent the explicit limitations of this Constitution. Section 10 — The Legislative Process 10.1 Introduction. Any member of either chamber may introduce a bill. Bills must address a single subject, stated clearly in their title. 10.2 Committee review. Each chamber shall establish standing committees with jurisdiction over defined subject areas. All bills shall be referred promptly to the relevant committee, and committees shall hold public hearings before reporting bills to the full chamber. A bill that is not reported within a reasonable time may be discharged to the floor by majority vote of the chamber under procedures established by law or chamber rule consistent with this Constitution. 10.3 Floor vote. A bill reported by committee shall be scheduled for a floor vote within a reasonable time. Floor debate shall be governed by rules established by each chamber, but no chamber rule may permit debate to be extended indefinitely to prevent a vote. Each chamber's rules shall provide a mechanism to close debate and proceed to a vote by majority will. 10.4 Passage. A bill passes a chamber upon approval by a majority of members present and voting, provided a quorum is present. A quorum consists of a majority of the full membership of the chamber. 10.5 Bicameral passage. A bill must pass both chambers in identical form before being presented to the President. If the chambers pass different versions of the same bill, a conference committee of members from both chambers shall attempt to reconcile the differences. A reconciled bill must pass both chambers before proceeding. 10.6 Presidential action. The President shall sign or veto a bill within 15 days of receiving it. If the President takes no action within 15 days, the bill becomes law. There is no pocket veto — a bill presented to the President during a congressional recess becomes law after 15 days if unsigned. 10.7 Single subject rule. No bill may address more than one subject. A bill that addresses multiple unrelated subjects is void in its entirety. Appropriations bills may fund multiple programs within the jurisdiction of a single subject area. 10.7A — Pre enactment single subject challenge. Any member of either chamber may file a single subject challenge with the Supreme Court at any point after a bill has passed both chambers and before the President acts on it. The Supreme Court shall rule on a single subject challenge within 15 days. If the Court finds a violation, the bill is returned to the originating chamber for severance or redrafting before proceeding to the President. The 15 day presidential action clock under Section 10.6 is tolled during any pending single subject challenge. Section 11 — Deadlock Resolution 11.1 If the Regional Assembly fails to pass a bill that has passed the House, or passes a materially different version, the following process applies: (a) The bill returns to the House, which may accept the Regional Assembly's amendments, reject them, or request a conference committee. (b) If a conference committee fails to produce an agreed version within 60 days, the original House passed version is put to a vote of the full Regional Assembly. (c) If the Regional Assembly votes against the original House version a second time, the bill does not proceed. It may be reintroduced in the next session. 11.2 — Delay rule. On any bill passed by the House that the Regional Assembly has not acted upon within the period established by law or chamber rule consistent with this Constitution, the House may by majority vote declare the bill delayed and request an immediate floor vote in the Regional Assembly. The deadlock resolution mechanism of Sections 11.1 and 11.2 may be limited by law or chamber rule to prevent repetitive use on the same bill within a single Congress. 11.3 — Regional Assembly Primacy on Appointments and Treaties. Sections 11.1 and 11.2 do not apply to confirmation of appointments or ratification of treaties, which are the exclusive power of the Regional Assembly and not subject to House override. Section 12 — The Budget Process 12.1 The President shall submit a proposed federal budget to Congress no later than the first Monday of February each year. 12.2 Congress shall consider and enact revenue, appropriations, and budget legislation on a timetable sufficient to preserve continuity of lawful government and democratic control of public finance. 12.3 Article XIV governs the constitutional principles of taxation and public revenue. Article XV governs borrowing, appropriations, debt, budget continuity, budget integrity, fiscal transparency, and classified spending. This Article allocates the legislative role in those processes. Section 12A — Classified Spending 12A.1 Congressional control of classified appropriations remains subject to Article XV. No secrecy designation may displace the House origination rule, lawful appropriations, or the oversight powers of Congress. 12A.2 Congress shall maintain oversight structures, secure reporting channels, and review procedures sufficient to carry out the classified spending safeguards established in Article XV. Section 13 — Supermajority Requirements The following actions require supermajority votes as specified: | Action | Threshold | | | | | Override presidential veto | 2/3 of both chambers | | Impeachment conviction (Regional Assembly) | 2/3 of Regional Assembly | | Expel a member | 2/3 of the relevant chamber | | Ratify a treaty | 2/3 of Regional Assembly | | Declare war against a non aggressor | 2/3 of both chambers | | Propose a constitutional amendment | 2/3 of both chambers | | Override an Electoral Commission ruling | Not permitted | | Remove a Constitutional Organ commissioner | 2/3 of both chambers plus judicial finding of cause | Section 14 — Congressional Ethics 14.1 Each chamber shall maintain an Ethics Committee responsible for investigating alleged violations of ethics rules by members of that chamber. 14.2 The Accountability Commission (Article XII) shall have independent jurisdiction to investigate federal officials, including members of Congress, for corruption, bribery, and abuse of office. The Accountability Commission's investigations supersede the jurisdiction of the relevant chamber's Ethics Committee in cases involving criminal conduct. 14.3 Members of Congress shall be subject to the financial disclosure, blind trust, and revolving door provisions of Article VI. 14.4 No member of Congress shall receive compensation for any service rendered, speech given, or article written in their capacity as a public official, beyond the compensation established by law. Section 15 — Minimum Session Requirement 15.1 Congress shall be in session for not fewer than 120 days per calendar year. Days in session are defined as days on which either chamber holds a recorded floor vote or a committee hearing open to the public record. Pro forma sessions in which no business is conducted do not count toward this requirement. 15.2 Failure to meet the 120 day requirement does not invalidate any legislation passed during the year, but shall be published by the Electoral Commission as a matter of public record and shall constitute a reportable failure in each member's official attendance record. 15.3 The 120 day floor may be raised but not lowered by statute. Section 15A — Mandatory Scheduling for Constitutionally Required Votes 15A.1 Where any provision of this Constitution requires a chamber of Congress to hold a recorded vote by a specific deadline, the following rules apply. Congress may by statute specify more detailed procedures consistent with this Section, but may not enact rules that make scheduling harder than the floor established here. (a) The presiding officer or majority leader of the relevant chamber shall place the required vote on the floor calendar in sufficient time for the vote to be held before the constitutional deadline. Congress shall by law establish the minimum advance notice required; absent such a law, the default is 5 days before the deadline. (b) If the required scheduling does not occur in time for the deadline to be met, any member of the chamber may file a mandatory scheduling motion requiring no committee referral, no rule waiver, and no leadership approval. The presiding officer shall bring that motion to a floor vote promptly; Congress shall by law establish the maximum time for this step, which may not exceed 48 hours. (c) A presiding officer or majority leader who prevents a constitutionally required vote from being scheduled without lawful justification is subject to removal from their leadership position by majority vote of the chamber and shall be referred to the Accountability Commission for investigation of obstruction of a constitutional process. (d) Any person with a direct legal interest in the outcome of a constitutionally required vote that has not been scheduled by the applicable deadline may seek a writ of mandamus in the United States District Court for the District of Columbia compelling the scheduling. Jurisdiction is exclusive to that court to prevent conflicting orders from multiple tribunals. The court shall rule within 5 days. Enforcement of the writ shall proceed through the court's ordinary contempt and enforcement powers. (e) A constitutionally required vote may not be defeated, delayed, or rendered impossible by adjournment, recess, failure to adopt a rule, refusal to recognize a motion, or suspension of ordinary legislative business. No internal rule, calendar practice, or leadership decision may extend the constitutional deadline. (f) Each chamber shall maintain by rule procedures sufficient to reconvene promptly, establish a public quorum, and hold any constitutionally required vote while in recess or during disruption of ordinary business. If those procedures are absent, suspended, or unavailable when a constitutional deadline approaches, the chamber shall meet under its last valid rules insofar as necessary to hold the required vote and record it publicly. 15A.2 This Section applies to all constitutionally required votes including but not limited to: emergency declaration approval under Article III Section 5.4; state representation suspension votes under Article X Section 1.6(c); war powers authorization under Article XVI Section 1.5(b); and impeachment votes under Article III Section 10.2A. It does not apply to ordinary legislation, appropriations, or other discretionary legislative business. 15A.3 This Section does not predetermine the outcome of any required vote if the vote is held on time. But where a constitutionally required vote is not held by the deadline, the default legal consequence specified in the governing Article takes effect automatically. Failure to hold the vote does not suspend, soften, or postpone any constitutional consequence that this Constitution otherwise makes automatic upon nonapproval, nonauthorization, lapse, or missed action. 15A.4 Illness, bereavement, family emergency, disaster, attack, official duty away from the seat of government, or comparable extenuating circumstance affecting individual members or officers does not extend a constitutional deadline, but neither shall such circumstances be treated as misconduct in themselves. Each chamber shall maintain humane continuity procedures, including emergency quorum rules and secure, publicly reviewable methods established by law for members unavoidably absent on account of such circumstances to participate in constitutionally required votes when feasible. In person voting remains the default. This Constitution does not require any particular technology or method of remote participation, but it does require continuity rules sufficient to prevent genuine disruption from defeating constitutionally required action. If a chamber, despite good faith use of those procedures, cannot hold the required vote because of widespread incapacity or genuinely impossible conditions, the default legal consequence specified in the governing Article still takes effect, but no individual sanction shall issue under this Section absent a finding of bad faith obstruction or refusal to use available continuity procedures. Coordinated absenteeism, coordinated refusal to use available continuity procedures, or other concerted conduct undertaken for the purpose of defeating a constitutionally required vote shall constitute obstruction of a constitutional process and is not excused by nominal reliance on personal hardship, travel, or ordinary chamber custom. Section 16 — Congressional Oversight 16.1 — The Oversight Power Congress possesses the power to investigate any matter within the scope of its legislative, appropriations, and confirmation authority, including the conduct of the executive branch, the administration of federal law, the expenditure of federal funds, and the activities of any person or entity subject to federal jurisdiction. The oversight power is an inherent and necessary attribute of the legislative function. No act of Congress, executive order, or claim of executive privilege may extinguish it. Oversight may be conducted through committee hearings, subpoenas, requests for documents, depositions, and any other lawful investigative means. The oversight power extends to past conduct as well as current operations. 16.2 — Subpoena Power and Expedited Judicial Enforcement Each chamber of Congress and each standing committee, acting by majority vote, may issue subpoenas compelling the testimony of witnesses and the production of documents, records, and other materials in connection with a lawful oversight inquiry. A subpoena issued under this Section is enforceable by federal courts. The following expedited process applies: (a) A committee that has issued a subpoena that has not been complied with within 30 days of service may apply to the United States District Court for the District of Columbia for an enforcement order (b) The court shall rule on the application within 30 days of filing. No continuance may extend this deadline except by order of the Chief Judge upon a finding of extraordinary circumstances (c) A person who fails to comply with a court enforcement order within 15 days is in contempt of court and subject to civil detention and daily fines until compliance (d) Civil detention under this Section is enforced by the United States Marshals Service and does not require action by the Department of Justice The expedited enforcement process of this Section may not be delayed, stayed, or extended by interlocutory appeal except upon a showing to the appellate court that the subpoena is facially unconstitutional. A claim of executive privilege does not automatically stay enforcement — it must be adjudicated under Section 16.6 on the same expedited timeline. Enforcement orders issued under subsection (b) are self executing upon issuance; the United States Marshals Service shall enforce them without awaiting further direction from the Department of Justice or any other executive agency. A Marshal who refuses or fails to enforce a valid subpoena enforcement order without lawful justification is subject to removal from office. If the Department of Justice refuses to support enforcement, the Accountability Commission (Article XII) is independently empowered to enforce congressional subpoenas through the same process, including by direct application to any United States District Court. 16.3 — Affirmative Duty to Testify The following officers of the executive branch have an affirmative constitutional duty to testify before Congress on matters within their official responsibilities when summoned by either chamber or by a committee with jurisdiction over those responsibilities: (a) All Cabinet secretaries and heads of executive departments (b) All heads of independent agencies and regulatory bodies (c) The Director of National Intelligence, the Director of the CIA, the Director of the FBI, and equivalent senior intelligence and law enforcement officials (d) Any other officer of the United States whose appointment requires Regional Assembly confirmation under this Constitution or federal law The duty to testify is distinct from the obligation to answer every question — a witness subject to this duty must appear, must answer questions not subject to a valid privilege claim under Section 16.6, and must do so truthfully. Refusal to appear without a valid legal basis, and refusal to answer questions not subject to a valid privilege claim, are each independent violations of this Section. A response of "I do not recall" or equivalent that a court subsequently finds was made in bad faith — that is, where the witness did in fact recall or had the means to recall with reasonable inquiry — constitutes perjury under Section 16.4. Witnesses subject to this duty have an affirmative obligation of reasonable inquiry before claiming lack of recall; a claim of non recall made without reasonable inquiry is deemed knowing if the information was available to the witness through records, staff, or ordinary professional practice. A violation of the duty to testify shall be referred by the relevant committee to the Accountability Commission for prosecution. An official who refuses to appear is subject to removal from office in addition to criminal penalties. 16.4 — Perjury and False Statements Before Congress No person appearing before either chamber of Congress, before any committee or subcommittee, or in any deposition conducted pursuant to a congressional subpoena, may knowingly make a false statement of material fact, knowingly deny material information, or knowingly present false documents. 16.4(a) — Jurisdiction. Perjury before Congress and knowing false statements in congressional proceedings shall be prosecuted exclusively by the Accountability Commission (Article XII). The Department of Justice has no jurisdiction over these offenses. This allocation exists because the executive branch controls the Department of Justice and has a structural conflict of interest in prosecuting those who lie to Congress about executive branch conduct. 16.4(b) — Penalties. A person convicted of perjury or making a knowing false statement before Congress shall be subject to: (i) Criminal penalties established by law (ii) If a current federal official: automatic removal from office upon conviction, without requiring a separate impeachment or chamber vote (iii) Permanent disqualification from holding any federal elected or appointed office (iv) No pardon — the President, and no other official with pardon authority, may pardon or commute a sentence for a violation of this Section. A pardon issued in violation of this prohibition is void. 16.4(c) — No statute of limitations. There is no statute of limitations for perjury before Congress or for knowing false statements in congressional proceedings. The passage of time does not extinguish the Accountability Commission's authority to prosecute. 16.4(d) — Scope. This Section applies regardless of whether the witness was placed under oath, provided that the witness was informed that truthful testimony was required. The formal oath requirement is a procedural safeguard, not a prerequisite for the obligation of truthfulness. 16.5 — Contempt of Congress A person who willfully refuses to comply with a valid congressional subpoena — whether to testify, to produce documents, or to appear — is in contempt of Congress. 16.5(a) — Referral. Contempt citations shall be referred by the relevant chamber to the Accountability Commission for criminal prosecution. The Department of Justice has no role in prosecuting contempt of Congress. This allocation applies for the same reason as Section 16.4(a) — the executive branch may not be the enforcer of congressional authority against executive branch witnesses. 16.5(b) — Penalties. Criminal contempt of Congress carries criminal penalties established by law and, for current federal officials, constitutes grounds for removal from office. The Accountability Commission shall prosecute within 90 days of referral unless the Commission publishes specific written findings that the evidence is insufficient to support prosecution. A general statement of insufficient resources or prosecutorial discretion without specific findings as to the evidence does not satisfy this requirement. 16.5(c) — Civil contempt. In addition to criminal referral, a chamber may seek civil enforcement of its subpoenas directly in federal court under Section 16.2. Civil and criminal contempt proceedings may proceed simultaneously. 16.6 — Executive Privilege: Definition, Scope, and Limits Executive privilege is a limited constitutional protection for specific categories of executive branch communications. It is not a general power to withhold information from Congress. The following rules govern its scope and exercise: 16.6(a) — Covered communications. Executive privilege protects: (i) Communications directly between the President and senior advisers regarding the President's own deliberative process on specific pending decisions, where disclosure would chill frank advice on that specific decision (ii) Information whose disclosure would compromise an active, ongoing criminal investigation or intelligence operation, upon a specific showing that disclosure would cause concrete harm to the investigation. The privilege lapses when the investigation is no longer active and may not be renewed indefinitely for the same matter. The Accountability Commission may audit the continued active status of any investigation claimed as the basis for privilege (iii) Genuine military operational secrets whose disclosure would directly endanger active operations or personnel 16.6(b) — Not covered. Executive privilege does not protect: (i) Evidence of criminal conduct, violations of this Constitution, or abuse of office by any official (ii) Information about actions that have already been taken and that Congress is investigating after the fact (iii) Communications that were not genuinely confidential at the time they occurred (iv) Information about the expenditure of appropriated funds (v) The identity of persons making or implementing decisions that Congress has authority to investigate (vi) Any communication that a court finds was made in furtherance of a crime or fraud 16.6(c) — How privilege is invoked. A claim of executive privilege must: (i) Be made in writing by the President personally, not by a subordinate official or agency counsel (ii) Identify with specificity each document or communication withheld and the basis for the claim (iii) Be filed with both the relevant committee and the Accountability Commission simultaneously A blanket claim of privilege over a category of documents without specific identification is void. 16.6(d) — Judicial review. A privilege claim is subject to judicial review under the expedited process of Section 16.2. A court shall examine the withheld materials in camera and rule within 30 days on whether each specific document falls within the covered categories of Section 16.6(a). A privilege claim covering documents that fall outside those categories shall be denied as to those documents. 16.6(e) — Congressional override. A 2/3 vote of the relevant committee may override a privilege claim and compel disclosure of specific materials where the committee finds that the public interest in disclosure substantially outweighs the executive interest in confidentiality. A congressional override is subject to judicial review, but the court shall give substantial deference to the committee's finding of public interest. In addition, the minority of a committee — comprising at least 1/3 of the committee's total membership — may force a floor vote on a privilege override by filing a written motion signed by a majority of all minority party members serving on the committee; upon such filing, the full chamber shall schedule a floor vote on whether to override the privilege claim, and that vote shall be held within 15 days of the filing being certified by the presiding officer of the chamber. A floor vote on privilege override requires the same 2/3 threshold applicable to committee overrides. 16.7 — Adverse Inference for Bad Faith Privilege Claims Where a court finds that a claim of executive privilege was asserted without a reasonable basis — that is, where the withheld materials plainly fall outside the categories of Section 16.6(a) or where the privilege was invoked primarily to delay or obstruct a congressional inquiry — the following consequences apply: (a) The relevant committee may draw an adverse inference from the withholding: it may treat the withheld materials as supporting the committee's inquiry in the most adverse manner consistent with other available evidence (b) The adverse inference is admissible in any subsequent congressional proceeding, report, or resolution (c) The official who asserted the bad faith privilege claim is referred to the Accountability Commission for investigation of obstruction of Congress The adverse inference provision creates a real cost for overclaiming privilege. An official who withholds documents knowing they are not privileged gains nothing — the committee may treat the withheld documents as confirming whatever the committee suspected, and the official faces obstruction proceedings. 16.8 — Protection for Congressional Witnesses Any federal official or employee who testifies truthfully before Congress is protected from any form of retaliation, including adverse employment action, reduction in responsibilities or clearances, threats or pressure to alter testimony, or any action directed at their family members in connection with their testimony. This protection applies from the date the official is subpoenaed or voluntarily agrees to testify. An official who is retaliated against may seek immediate injunctive relief in any federal court. The Accountability Commission shall investigate and prosecute retaliation; a finding of retaliation constitutes grounds for removal of the retaliating official. This Section does not protect false testimony. 16.9 — Grants of Immunity Either chamber, or a committee by majority vote with approval of the full chamber, may grant use immunity to a witness compelling their testimony before Congress. Immunized testimony and evidence derived directly from it may not be used in a criminal prosecution of the witness, but independent prosecution using separately gathered evidence remains available. The purpose is to enable Congress to obtain truthful information while preserving the possibility of independent criminal accountability. 16.10 — No Interference with Oversight No executive order, presidential directive, agency rule, or congressional leadership decision may direct a federal official to withhold truthful information from a congressional oversight inquiry, to refuse to appear before Congress when lawfully summoned, or to provide Congress with information the official knows to be false or misleading. An order directing any of these acts is void, and compliance with such an order is not a defense to liability under this Section. An official who receives such an order shall report it to the Accountability Commission within 72 hours.

docs.title: Article III — The Executive
docs.summary: Establishes the structure, powers, and limits of the executive branch. The President is both head of state and head of government. Executive power is derived from this Constitution and may not be exercised beyond its limits regardless of congressional authorization.

docs.search_text: Article III — The Executive Status: [ DRAFT ] Scope Establishes the structure, powers, and limits of the executive branch. The President is both head of state and head of government. Executive power is derived from this Constitution and may not be exercised beyond its limits regardless of congressional authorization. Section 1 — The President 1.1 The executive power of the United States is vested in a President of the United States of America. 1.2 The President shall: (a) Faithfully execute the laws of the United States (b) Serve as commander in chief of the armed forces (c) Represent the United States in foreign affairs (d) Nominate officers of the United States as provided in this Constitution (e) Report to Congress on the state of the nation no less than once per year (f) Convene either or both chambers of Congress in extraordinary session when necessary 1.3 The President shall take the following oath before assuming office, administered by the Chief Justice of the Supreme Court: "I do solemnly affirm that I will faithfully execute the office of President of the United States, will to the best of my ability preserve, protect, and defend the Constitution of the United States, and will ensure that the laws of the United States are faithfully carried out." Section 2 — Eligibility and Term 2.1 Qualifications. A person is eligible to serve as President if they: (a) Are at least 35 years of age at the time of taking office (b) Have been a citizen of the United States for at least 14 years (c) Have been a resident of the United States for at least 7 of the 14 years preceding election 2.2 The requirement of natural born citizenship is abolished. No distinction shall be drawn between citizens by birth and citizens by naturalization for purposes of eligibility for the presidency. The general principles of equal citizenship and equal eligibility for office are governed by Article IX. 2.2A — Sole citizenship required. The President must hold only United States citizenship while in office. A person who holds citizenship of any other nation at the time of election must formally renounce that citizenship before taking the oath of office. A President who acquires or accepts citizenship of a foreign nation during their term is immediately disqualified and the succession provisions of Section 12 apply. This requirement applies to the Vice President on the same terms. 2.2B — Prior foreign government service. No person who has served as an officer, official, elected representative, or employee of a foreign government, foreign military, or foreign intelligence service within the twenty years preceding their election may serve as President or Vice President. Service as a private citizen in a foreign country, employment by a foreign private company, or academic or cultural exchange do not constitute foreign government service for purposes of this Section. The Electoral Commission shall make an initial administrative determination of compliance as part of the candidate qualification process under Section 2.2C, subject to expedited judicial review. 2.2C — Enhanced foreign ties disclosure. Every candidate for President or Vice President shall file a complete foreign ties disclosure with the Electoral Commission no later than the date they qualify for ballot access. The disclosure shall include: (a) All citizenship or nationality held currently or within the preceding 20 years, and any renunciations made (b) All income received from foreign governments, foreign state owned enterprises, or entities controlled by foreign governments within the preceding 10 years (c) All business interests, equity holdings, contracts, or debts involving foreign governments or entities owned or controlled by foreign governments within the preceding 10 years (d) All prior service with any foreign government, military, or intelligence service, regardless of how long ago it occurred (e) All immediate family members who currently hold positions in a foreign government or military The Electoral Commission shall publish all disclosures publicly within 5 days of receipt. A candidate who files a materially false or incomplete disclosure is disqualified from the ballot and subject to criminal prosecution under Article VI; such false or materially incomplete disclosure is an absolute bar to certification, not merely a post election remedy. The Electoral Commission shall verify disclosures against available federal intelligence and law enforcement records before ballot qualification is granted, not merely before final certification of election results. Any candidate aggrieved by an administrative disqualification under this Section has the right to expedited review in the United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit, which shall issue a ruling within 14 days. 2.3 Term. The President shall serve a term of four years, beginning on the twentieth day of January following their election. 2.4 Term limits. No person may be elected President more than twice. Terms need not be consecutive — a person who has served two terms may seek the presidency again after sitting out at least one full term. No person who has served more than two years of another President's term may be elected more than once thereafter. 2.4A — Vice Presidential terms count toward the limit. Any full term served as Vice President counts as one term toward the two term presidential limit. A person who has served two full terms as Vice President may not be elected President. A person who has served one full term as Vice President may be elected President once. A partial Vice Presidential term of fewer than two years does not count. This provision prevents the use of the Vice Presidency as a device to extend executive influence beyond the two term limit intended by this Section. 2.5 The President shall receive a fixed compensation set by law at the beginning of each presidential term. This compensation may not be increased or decreased during the term for which it was set. The President may not receive any other emolument, payment, gift, or benefit from the United States or any state, foreign government, or private entity during their term, except compensation established by law. Section 3 — The Vice President 3.1 The Vice President shall be elected jointly with the President on the same ticket, as provided in Article I. The Vice President assumes the powers and duties of the President upon the President's death, resignation, removal, or incapacity, as provided in Sections 11 and 12 of this Article. 3.2 The Vice President shall preside over the Regional Assembly but shall cast no vote except to break a tie. 3.3 The Vice President shall serve as a member of the Cabinet and shall assume such responsibilities as the President delegates. 3.4 The Vice President must meet the same eligibility requirements as the President. 3.5 If the Vice Presidency becomes vacant, the President shall nominate a new Vice President, subject to confirmation by a majority vote of both chambers of Congress in joint session. The nominee must be confirmed within 60 days. Section 4 — The Cabinet and Executive Departments 4.1 The President may establish executive departments and agencies to carry out the functions of the executive branch. The heads of executive departments shall form the Cabinet. 4.2 Confirmation required. All Cabinet members, heads of independent agencies, and senior officers of the United States must be confirmed by the Regional Assembly before assuming their duties. No person may exercise the powers of a position requiring confirmation without having been confirmed, except as provided in Section 4.3. 4.3 Acting officials. When a confirmed position becomes vacant, the President may designate an acting official to serve temporarily. An acting official may not serve for more than 45 days without a confirmed successor being nominated and submitted to the Regional Assembly. A nomination not acted upon by the Regional Assembly within 90 days of submission is deemed confirmed. No person who has been rejected by the Regional Assembly may serve as acting official in the same position. 4.4 Cabinet members serve at the pleasure of the President and may be removed at any time without cause, except that removal during an active investigation by the Accountability Commission into the President or Cabinet members requires a written statement of reasons published within 48 hours of removal. 4.5 Independent agencies. The heads of independent regulatory agencies whose mandates require insulation from political direction — including but not limited to financial regulators, consumer protection agencies, and communications regulators — may be removed by the President only for cause. Cause shall mean demonstrated neglect of duty, malfeasance, incapacity, or conviction of a crime. Congress shall designate by law which agencies are independent for purposes of this Section. Section 5 — Emergency Powers 5.1 Declaration. The President may declare a national emergency in response to an imminent threat to public safety, national security, or critical national infrastructure that requires executive action faster than the ordinary legislative process permits. 5.2 Immediate powers. Upon a valid declaration of emergency, the President may: (a) Direct federal agencies to use existing appropriated funds within their lawful mandates to address the emergency (b) Mobilize the National Guard and federal emergency services (c) Suspend specific regulatory requirements that materially impede emergency response, for the duration of the emergency only (d) Exercise any additional emergency powers specifically authorized by law 5.3 Limits. No emergency declaration may: (a) Suspend or restrict the right to vote, delay or cancel elections, or alter the electoral process (b) Suspend habeas corpus except as provided in Section 5.7 (c) Direct the armed forces against citizens on domestic soil without congressional authorization (d) Authorize surveillance of citizens without judicial warrant (e) Appropriate funds not previously authorized by Congress (f) Override any provision of Article V (Rights and Freedoms) except as permitted by Article V itself 5.4 Congressional approval. An emergency declaration must be submitted to the Regional Assembly within 48 hours. The Regional Assembly must approve or reject the declaration within 30 days. The Regional Assembly approval vote is a constitutionally required vote subject to the mandatory scheduling and continuity rules of Article II Section 15A. Recess, adjournment, or failure of internal chamber procedure does not suspend that deadline.
