Where a full conflict cannot be avoided, courts shall consider: (a) The relative weight of the interests at stake for each right holder (b) Whether one right directly enables the exercise of another (the more foundational right is given greater weight) (c) The proportionality of any limitation imposed on one right to protect another 16.2 No right in this Article shall be interpreted to permit the use of that right as a weapon to destroy the rights of others.

docs.title: Article VI — Democratic Integrity
docs.summary: Protects the democratic system itself from subversion, foreign interference, and capture of the information environment. This Article has no equivalent in the prior Constitution — it treats bad-faith actors as a design constraint rather than an anomaly. It establishes the rules governing electoral subversion, foreign meddling in elections and politics, and media plurality. Enforcement is primarily through the Constitutional Organs established in Article XII.

headings.text: Section 1 — Anti-Subversion and Protection of Democratic Processes
Section 2 — Campaign Finance and Political Money
Section 3 — Corruption, Conflicts of Interest, and Self-Dealing
5.4 — Prohibition on acting on foreign electoral assistance
5.5 — Shell entities and covert foreign political influence
Section 7 — Media Pluralism and Information Integrity

docs.search_text: Article VI — Democratic Integrity Status: [ DRAFT ] Scope Protects the democratic system itself from subversion, foreign interference, and capture of the information environment. This Article has no equivalent in the prior Constitution — it treats bad faith actors as a design constraint rather than an anomaly. It establishes the rules governing electoral subversion, foreign meddling in elections and politics, and media plurality. Enforcement is primarily through the Constitutional Organs established in Article XII. Government ethics — corruption, financial conflicts of interest, civil service protection, lobbying, and financial market integrity — is in Article VIII — Government Ethics . Campaign finance is in Article VII — Campaign Finance . Section 1 — Anti Subversion and Protection of Democratic Processes 1.1 — The Core Prohibition No person shall, by force, fraud, coercion, or abuse of official authority, attempt to prevent, obstruct, or reverse the lawful conduct of a federal election, the counting or certification of votes, or the constitutional transfer of power following a federal election. 1.2 — Definition of Subversion For purposes of this Article, subverting a lawful election or constitutional transfer of power includes: (a) Using or directing the use of force, threat of force, or organized intimidation to prevent voters from voting, prevent votes from being counted, or prevent certified results from taking effect (b) Using official authority — including military command, law enforcement direction, or control of government agencies — to obstruct, delay, or falsify the counting or certification of votes (c) Deliberately destroying, altering, concealing, or fabricating election records, ballots, or certification documents (d) Directing or coordinating with foreign governments or foreign nationals to manipulate election outcomes, in violation of Section 5 (e) Refusing as a public official to perform a mandatory legal duty in the certification or transfer of power process, where that refusal is designed to prevent or delay a lawful transfer. A public official who fails to perform a mandatory electoral duty within the required timeframe is presumed to have acted with intent to subvert; this presumption may be rebutted only by clear and convincing evidence of genuine incapacity. (f) Organizing or participating in a conspiracy to achieve any of the above 1.3 — What Is Not Subversion The following do not constitute subversion under this Article, regardless of their political effect: (a) Filing lawsuits, petitions, or legal challenges to election results through lawful judicial processes (b) Requesting recounts, audits, or reviews through lawful administrative processes (c) Publicly claiming that an election was fraudulent or unfair, including through political speech, press releases, or media appearances. The speech protection in this subsection does not extend to false statements of fact about electoral outcomes made with actual knowledge of their falsity or with reckless disregard for their truth, where such statements are made in coordination with a broader subversion effort as defined in §1.2. (d) Peaceful protest, demonstration, or assembly regarding election outcomes (e) Lobbying election officials, legislators, or courts through lawful means Subversion requires going outside lawful processes — using force, fraud, or coercion rather than legal and political challenge. 1.4 — Penalties Any person who violates Section 1.1 shall: (a) Be permanently disqualified from holding any federal or state public office, appointed or elected, from the date of a judicial finding of violation. Disqualification is immediate upon conviction and does not require a separate proceeding. (b) Be subject to criminal prosecution under federal law. Congress shall establish criminal penalties for violations of this Section. No statute of limitations shall apply to offenses under this Section. (c) Be ineligible to receive a pardon for any offense arising from the violation. No President, Governor, or other official with pardon authority may pardon or commute a sentence for a violation of this Section. A pardon issued in violation of this prohibition is void. 1.5 — Scope of Application This Section applies to: All federal and state elected officials All appointed federal officials All members of the armed forces and federal law enforcement All private persons who conspire with officials to commit the above acts Candidates for federal office, from the date they file for candidacy 1.6 — Adjudication Charges under this Section shall be prosecuted by the Accountability Commission (Article XII). The Accountability Commission's authority to prosecute under this Section cannot be overridden, transferred, or terminated by the President, Congress, or any other official. Section 2 — Campaign Finance and Political Money The constitutional framework governing political money, disclosure, public financing, coordination, source restrictions, and campaign finance enforcement is established in Article VII — Campaign Finance and Political Money . Nothing in this Article limits the operation of Article VII. Section 3 — Corruption, Conflicts of Interest, and Self Dealing The constitutional rules governing corruption, financial conflicts of interest, self dealing, and related misconduct by federal officials are established in Article VIII — Government Ethics . Section 3A — Financial Market Integrity The constitutional rules governing financial market integrity, including the special safeguards applicable to market regulators and officeholders with market moving authority, are established in Article VIII — Government Ethics . Section 4 — The Civil Service The constitutional rules governing the civil service, merit protection, and retaliation against public employees are established in Article VIII — Government Ethics . Section 5 — Foreign Interference 5.1 — Prohibition on foreign electoral interference No foreign government, foreign national, foreign corporation, or entity owned or controlled by foreign interests shall: (a) Contribute money or anything of value to any federal election campaign or political committee (b) Make expenditures designed to influence any federal election (c) Direct, coordinate, or communicate with any candidate, campaign, or political committee regarding strategy, messaging, or operations in connection with a federal election (d) Fund political advertising, social media campaigns, or other communications designed to influence federal elections 5.2 — Disclosure of foreign contacts All candidates for federal office, and all senior campaign officials, shall report to the Electoral Commission within 24 hours any offer, communication, or meeting involving a foreign government or foreign national relating to the campaign, any election, or any matter of federal policy. Failure to disclose is a criminal offense. Any campaign official who fails to report within 24 hours is individually liable for that failure regardless of whether the campaign itself reports. 5.3 — Foreign lobbying All persons acting as agents of a foreign government or foreign political party in connection with political activities within the United States shall register with the Accountability Commission and publicly disclose their activities, clients, and compensation on a quarterly basis. Failure to register is a criminal offense. 5.4 — Prohibition on acting on foreign electoral assistance No candidate or campaign official who receives an offer of foreign assistance in connection with a federal election may accept, act upon, or fail to report that offer. Receipt of foreign electoral assistance without reporting it is a violation of Section 1.1 of this Article. Receiving a foreign offer and failing to report it within 24 hours constitutes "acting upon" it for purposes of this Section. Willful blindness as to the foreign origin of an offer is not a defense. 5.5 — Shell entities and covert foreign political influence No person or entity may serve as a conduit to route foreign political funding, direction, or sponsorship in a manner that would be prohibited under this Section if engaged in directly by a foreign government, foreign national, or foreign controlled entity. The domestic legal form of an intermediary does not determine whether the underlying activity constitutes foreign political interference under this Article. Congress shall require disclosure of the ultimate beneficial sponsor of mass political communications where there is reasonable basis to believe the communication is funded, directed, or materially sponsored by a foreign source. This requirement extends to political communications beyond formal campaign expenditures where those communications are directed at the domestic public on matters of federal governance, elections, or constitutional legitimacy. Section 6 — Lobbying and Access The constitutional rules governing lobbying, access, and related disclosure obligations are established in Article VIII — Government Ethics . Section 7 — Media Pluralism and Information Integrity 7.1 — Ownership concentration limits Dangerous concentration of media ownership in too few hands undermines the diversity of information and viewpoint essential to democratic self governance. Congress shall establish by law limits on the share of national media audience that any single person, corporation, or entity under common ownership or control may reach across all media formats. Congress shall set these limits at levels that ensure genuine plurality of ownership and viewpoint, review them not less than every ten years, and define how audience reach is measured. The specific thresholds are legislative, not constitutional, so they may be updated as the media landscape changes without requiring a constitutional amendment. "National media audience reach" means the aggregate unduplicated weekly audience across all platforms, measured by a methodology established by the independent media regulator. The regulator shall publish the methodology and all underlying data. The cap set by Congress may not exceed 30% of the national audience. 7.2 — Local market limits No single entity may own more than one television broadcast station, one daily newspaper, and one radio station serving the same local market. Congress may establish stricter limits by law. 7.3 — Foreign ownership No foreign government, foreign national, or entity owned or controlled by foreign interests may own or exercise editorial control over any broadcast media outlet or major digital platform operating in the United States. This prohibition applies regardless of the form of ownership, the degree of foreign control, or whether the foreign interest is held directly or through domestic intermediaries. Congress shall establish by statute the thresholds at which foreign ownership is deemed controlling and the procedures for divestiture. The constitutional principle — no foreign control of domestic media — is fixed; the specific ownership percentage thresholds are statutory. For purposes of this Section, foreign "control" means ownership of 10% or more of voting shares, the right to appoint any member of the board of directors, or the ability to direct editorial policy through contract, debt covenant, or any other arrangement. Congress may set lower thresholds by statute but may not set higher ones. 7.4 — Public broadcasting A system of public broadcasting shall be funded through a dedicated mechanism insulated from annual appropriations, to ensure it cannot be defunded as political pressure. Public broadcasting shall operate under an editorial independence charter that prohibits direction by any elected or appointed official. Its leadership shall be appointed by a process that prevents domination by any single political party. No more than 40% of the members of the public broadcasting governing board may be affiliated with any single political party. For purposes of this Section, affiliation is established by party registration, political donations above $500 in the prior five years, or public endorsement of a partisan candidate within the prior two years. 7.5 — Algorithmic transparency Major digital platforms that use algorithmic systems to determine the distribution of political news, political advertising, or content related to elections shall: (a) Disclose to the Electoral Commission the criteria and weighting used by their algorithms in distributing such content (b) Submit to independent audits of their systems for discriminatory or manipulative amplification (c) Provide equal access to advertising tools and distribution for all lawfully registered political candidates and parties 7.5A — Dominant civic intermediaries Congress may by law impose neutral continuity, nondiscrimination, audit, and emergency carriage duties on dominant private intermediaries whose services are effectively indispensable to lawful federal political participation, election administration, or the distribution of official emergency information. Such duties may be imposed only where: (a) the intermediary provides infrastructure or distribution services so concentrated that coordinated denial or manipulation would materially impair meaningful federal political competition, election administration, or access to official emergency information (b) the duties are viewpoint neutral, publicly stated in advance, and reviewable on an expedited basis by the Electoral Commission and the federal courts (c) the duties do not authorize general control of lawful private editorial judgment or compelled ideological speech beyond what is strictly necessary to preserve fair electoral competition, election administration, or access to official emergency information During a declared constitutional emergency, a federal election period, or a federally certified election administration disruption, Congress may authorize the Electoral Commission to order temporary corrective relief under such laws, including restoration of nondiscriminatory access, suspension of manipulative visibility practices affecting official emergency or election administration information, and preservation of service sufficient to prevent coordinated exclusion of lawful qualified political actors. 7.6 — Enforcement Congress shall establish an independent regulatory body with authority to enforce media ownership limits. This body shall be structured as an independent agency under Article XIII, removable only for cause. Section 8 — Enforcement 8.1 The independent media regulator established under Section 7.6 shall be structured so that its commissioners are removable only for cause. "Cause" for removal means criminal conviction, a serious ethics violation, or incapacity to perform the duties of office. A commissioner may not be removed on the basis of the content of their regulatory decisions. The regulator shall be funded by fees assessed on entities it regulates and shall not depend on annual congressional appropriation. The provisions of this Article are enforced by: The Accountability Commission (Article XII) for anti subversion and foreign interference provisions The Electoral Commission (Article XII) for campaign finance and election integrity provisions (see Article VII) The independent media regulator (Section 7.6) for media ownership provisions See Article VIII for enforcement of government ethics, civil service, and lobbying provisions 8.2 Neither the President, Congress, nor any court may direct either Constitutional Organ in the handling of individual cases under this Article. The Accountability Commission's prosecutorial independence under this Article is part of the unamendable core of Article XI. 8.3 Any person — including a sitting President, member of Congress, or federal judge — is subject to investigation and prosecution under this Article. No office confers immunity from the provisions of this Article. A sitting President under investigation retains the ability to perform official duties but may not direct or obstruct the investigation.

docs.title: Article VII — Campaign Finance and Political Money
docs.summary: Establishes the constitutional framework governing money in federal and state elections. The core principles are that voters have a right to know who is funding political power, that political office must remain realistically accessible without wealth or large private donors, and that legislatures may regulate political money to prevent corruption, domination, concealment, and foreign influence.

headings.text: Section 3 — Spending Limits and Outside Spending
Section 5 — Transition, Inaugural, and Post-Election Money

docs.search_text: Article VII — Campaign Finance and Political Money Status: [ DRAFT ] Scope Establishes the constitutional framework governing money in federal and state elections. The core principles are that voters have a right to know who is funding political power, that political office must remain realistically accessible without wealth or large private donors, and that legislatures may regulate political money to prevent corruption, domination, concealment, and foreign influence. Section 1 — Constitutional Authority and Disclosure 1.1 — Authority to regulate political money Congress shall have power to regulate, limit, and require disclosure of contributions and expenditures made for the purpose of influencing federal elections. Money spent to influence elections is subject to regulation regardless of the legal form through which it is spent. No judicial doctrine shall be understood to forbid reasonable regulation of political spending by corporations, organizations, or other non individual entities on the theory that such spending is categorically immune from regulation as protected speech. Regulations on individual political spending remain subject to strict scrutiny. Regulations on political spending by non individual entities need only serve a substantial government interest. 1.2 — Traceable disclosure required All contributions and expenditures made for the purpose of influencing a federal election shall be publicly disclosed. Disclosure must identify the original human source of the funds. No entity, trust, account, intermediary, nominee, or pass through arrangement may be used to obscure that source. If the original human source cannot be identified, the contribution or expenditure is prohibited except for nominal amounts below a threshold fixed by law. Congress and the Electoral Commission shall provide by law for enforcement of this requirement through chains of intermediaries. 1.3 — Timely public notice Significant contributions and expenditures must be disclosed promptly enough for voters to know their source before casting ballots. Congress may set thresholds and deadlines by law, but may not permit delayed disclosure that defeats the purpose of meaningful pre election transparency. 1.4 — Nonprofit, pass through, and affiliated source transparency An organization may not use tax status, pass through status, or layered entity form to conceal the original human source of political funds. Congress and the Electoral Commission may require that affiliated or coordinated sources be treated as a single source and that disclosure requirements not be defeated by fragmentation. 1.5 — Coordination and acting in concert An expenditure is not independent if it is made in coordination with a candidate, campaign, party, or their agents. Congress and the Electoral Commission may define coordination by law, establish rebuttable presumptions to prevent disguised coordination, and treat coordinated or concerted spending as a single source for purposes of limits, caps, and disclosure. 1.6 — Foreign money prohibited No foreign national, foreign government, foreign corporation, or entity owned or controlled by foreign interests may contribute to or make expenditures in connection with any federal, state, or local election in the United States, whether directly or through a domestic intermediary. Congress and the Electoral Commission may take all measures necessary to enforce this prohibition, including treating willful blindness as a violation. 1.7 — Entity spending and source restrictions Congress may regulate or prohibit political spending by corporations, labor organizations, trade associations, contractors, pass through entities, tax exempt entities, and other legal forms where necessary to prevent corruption, concealment, coercion, or circumvention. Congress may also regulate in kind political contributions, digital asset political spending, and any other transfer of value used to influence an election. Section 2 — Public Financing 2.1 — Competitive public financing required Congress shall maintain a system of public financing for federal elections sufficient to permit a well organized candidate to run a competitive campaign for federal office without reliance on large private donors. Participation by candidates may be voluntary, but any candidate who accepts public financing may be required to accept reasonable spending conditions as a condition of participation. The system shall include matching or equivalent support for small dollar contributions and shall be funded through a mechanism insulated from ordinary annual political retaliation. 2.2 — Parameter setting and incumbency neutrality Congress may establish the constitutional floors and ceilings of the public financing system and broader campaign finance framework, but the specific dollar amounts, contribution limits, qualifying thresholds, matching ratios, and comparable numerical parameters shall be set or adjusted by the Electoral Commission within those bounds. The system shall be designed and administered so that a challenger with demonstrated grassroots support has a realistic path to qualifying for and competing on substantially fair terms with an incumbent. Courts may enforce this principle and the Electoral Commission shall periodically review whether the system satisfies it. 2.3 — Adjustment and adequacy Campaign finance thresholds and public financing parameters shall be periodically adjusted for inflation or other objective changes in economic conditions. Law shall provide for review of whether the public financing system remains genuinely competitive and for response to any finding of inadequacy. 2.4 — Self financing and legislative self dealing Congress may regulate the effects of extreme candidate self financing, including by providing responsive public support to opponents who rely on the public financing system. No chamber of Congress may unilaterally set the specific campaign finance parameters governing elections to that same chamber. Congress shall provide by law for conflict resistant parameter setting consistent with this Article. Section 3 — Spending Limits and Outside Spending 3.1 — Outside spending may be limited Congress may limit total outside spending in connection with a federal race, across federal races, or both. It may distinguish between candidate spending, party spending, and outside spending, and may exempt bona fide news reporting, editorial activity, and other journalistic work. Law may define the election periods and forms of advocacy subject to such limits, provided those definitions are directed to real electoral influence and not evaded by formal wording tests alone. 3.2 — Single candidate and concentrated outside spending Outside spending that is substantially directed toward the election or defeat of a single identifiable candidate may be treated as presumptively coordinated and therefore subject to contribution limits and anti coordination rules. Congress may also limit the share of total outside spending in a race or geography that may be supplied by a single actor or by actors acting in concert, in order to prevent domination of a race by concentrated wealth or by formally separate but practically unified spenders. 3.3 — Pre election restraint and anti evasion Congress may impose heightened disclosure, spending restrictions, or short blackout periods immediately before an election where necessary to preserve informed voting and prevent last minute concealment or circumvention. Spending outside formal election windows may still be regulated under this Article when it is part of a unified electoral effort or is used to evade otherwise lawful limits. Section 4 — Source Restrictions 4.1 — Contractors and comparable dependency relationships Congress may prohibit or limit political contributions and expenditures by federal contractors and by entities in comparable financial relationships with the federal government where necessary to prevent pay to play corruption or its appearance. 4.2 — Anonymous digital asset spending prohibited Congress may prohibit political contributions and expenditures made through any means by which the original human source of funds cannot be reliably identified and disclosed, regardless of the technological form. 4.3 — In kind value covered Non monetary transfers of value made for the purpose of influencing an election are contributions for purposes of this Article and are subject to the same disclosure, source, and anti circumvention rules as monetary transfers. Section 5 — Transition, Inaugural, and Post Election Money 5.1 — Presidential transition funding The presidential transition is a governmental function and shall be funded only as provided by law from public funds. No private contribution, donation, gift, or thing of value may be accepted for transition operations. Campaign funds may not be converted to transition use. 5.2 — Inaugural and post election fundraising Congress may permit private fundraising for inaugural ceremonies or comparable post election purposes only subject to the disclosure, source, segregation, and anti corruption rules of this Article. Campaign debt retirement, leadership political committees, and comparable post election political fundraising remain subject to the contribution limits, disclosure rules, and source restrictions of this Article. 5.3 — Political advertising transparency Congress and the Electoral Commission shall maintain a public record of political advertising sufficient to disclose its source, content, and material reach before and after each election. Section 6 — Enforcement 6.1 — Administration and prosecution The Electoral Commission shall administer and civilly enforce this Article. The Accountability Commission shall prosecute willful criminal violations. Congress may establish tiered civil and criminal enforcement consistent with Article XII. Willful concealment of political funding sources, willful use of shell entities or comparable devices to evade disclosure, willful laundering of prohibited political funds, and willful acceptance or use of foreign political money are serious offenses and may be punished accordingly. 6.2 — Timely remedies Congress shall provide by law for expedited remedies sufficient to prevent unlawful political money from shaping an election before voters can know its source. Section 7 — State Elections Floor The minimum campaign finance standards applicable to state and local elections are established in Article X Section 1.7. This Article's full framework applies to federal elections. States may adopt equivalent or stricter standards for their own elections and may access federal campaign finance infrastructure where this Constitution or federal law so provides. See Article XII for Electoral Commission structure and enforcement framework.

docs.title: Article VIII — Government Ethics
docs.summary: Establishes the ethics rules that govern how federal officials behave in office. These provisions address corruption, financial conflicts of interest, civil service merit protection, lobbying, and the integrity of financial markets. Enforcement is primarily through the Accountability Commission (Article XII). Democratic integrity provisions — anti-subversion, foreign interference in elections, and media pluralism — are in Article VI. General constitutional rules governing the structure, classification, and independence of federal agencies are in Article XIII; this Article states the substantive ethics and market-integrity requirements that those agencies and officials must obey.

headings.text: Section 1 — Corruption, Conflicts of Interest, and Self-Dealing
1.8 — Permitted investments: the index fund rule
1.13 — Beneficial ownership disclosure for federal contractors
1.14 — Prohibition on use of government resources for campaign purposes
2.2 — Independence of the financial market regulator
2.5 — No settlement without admission for major fraud

docs.search_text: Article VIII — Government Ethics Status: [ DRAFT ] Scope Establishes the ethics rules that govern how federal officials behave in office. These provisions address corruption, financial conflicts of interest, civil service merit protection, lobbying, and the integrity of financial markets. Enforcement is primarily through the Accountability Commission (Article XII). Democratic integrity provisions — anti subversion, foreign interference in elections, and media pluralism — are in Article VI. General constitutional rules governing the structure, classification, and independence of federal agencies are in Article XIII; this Article states the substantive ethics and market integrity requirements that those agencies and officials must obey. Section 1 — Corruption, Conflicts of Interest, and Self Dealing 1.1 — Financial disclosure All federal officials shall file complete financial disclosures covering their assets, liabilities, income sources, and financial interests at the beginning of service, at regular intervals during service, and upon any material change. Disclosures shall be made public promptly as provided by law. Failure to file, or filing a materially false disclosure, is grounds for removal and prosecution. 1.2 — Blind trusts Any federal official whose financial holdings create a conflict of interest with the duties of their office shall place those holdings in a blind trust managed by an independent trustee. The official shall have no communication with the trustee regarding investment decisions during their service. 1.3 — Emoluments No federal official shall accept any gift, payment, benefit, or thing of value from a foreign government or its agents, or from any domestic entity with a direct financial interest in the official's decisions, beyond reasonable compensation for actual services rendered in a non official capacity and disclosed as required by Section 1.1. Accepting a prohibited emolument is grounds for removal and criminal prosecution. 1.4 — Bribery and self dealing No federal official shall take any official action — vote, decision, appointment, contract, regulation, or enforcement — in exchange for anything of value given to themselves, their family, or any entity in which they have a financial interest. Self dealing shall be defined broadly to include: awarding contracts to entities in which the official holds interests; making decisions that benefit relatives who have not been confirmed to their positions through normal processes; and using official information for personal financial gain. 1.5 — Revolving door No former federal official may, for a substantial period set by law and not less than five years, work for, consult for, lobby on behalf of, or accept compensation from any private entity that was directly regulated or substantially affected by decisions they made or policies they supervised during their service. Congress may extend but not reduce this period. 1.6 — Nepotism No federal official may appoint, hire, promote, or advocate for the appointment of a family member to any position within the same agency, department, or branch of government in which the official serves. 1.7 — Automatic removal A federal official convicted of bribery, self dealing, or an emoluments violation shall be automatically removed from office upon conviction. Removal does not require a separate impeachment proceeding for officials ordinarily subject to impeachment, though impeachment may proceed concurrently. 1.8 — Permitted investments: the index fund rule No federal official, and no spouse, domestic partner, or dependent child of a federal official, may hold investments that create a substantial risk of conflict with public duties while the official is in office. Congress shall enforce this rule through a system that: (a) Permits broadly diversified funds, government obligations, a primary residence, and other holdings unlikely to distort official judgment (b) Prohibits individual securities, sector specific instruments, and comparable concentrated interests that would create incentives to favor particular firms, industries, or transactions (c) Requires divestiture, recusal, blind trust treatment, or other effective safeguards for illiquid or pre existing holdings that cannot immediately be converted The purpose of this Section is to ensure that federal officials have no personal financial incentive to favor any company, sector, or industry over another in the exercise of their official duties. 1.9 — Divestiture upon taking office A federal official who holds prohibited investments at the time of taking office shall divest them within a reasonable period set by law. Congress shall provide tax deferral or equivalent treatment for mandatory divestitures so that compliance is not penalized. 1.10 — Real time disclosure of all trades All purchases, sales, and transfers of investments by a federal official or their covered family members shall be disclosed promptly to the Accountability Commission and made public as provided by law. Late or false disclosure is a violation regardless of whether the trade itself was permitted. 1.11 — Penalties for investment violations A federal official or covered family member who holds a prohibited investment or executes a prohibited trade shall be subject to disgorgement, substantial civil penalty, and, for willful violation, criminal prosecution. Serious or repeated violations are grounds for removal. No federal official or covered family member may retain financial profit derived from non public information obtained through public office. 1.12 — Scope of application The investment rules of Sections 1.8 through 1.11 apply to: All members of Congress The President, Vice President, and all Cabinet members All federal judges at every level All senior executive branch officials confirmed by the Regional Assembly Senior congressional staff, defined as chiefs of staff, staff directors of committees, and legislative directors, who have access to non public legislative information Spouses, domestic partners, and dependent children of all of the above while the official is serving The rules apply from the date the official takes office or, for staff, from the date of appointment to a covered position. Congress may extend reasonable post service rules where necessary to prevent evasion. 1.13 — Beneficial ownership disclosure for federal contractors Congress shall require entities seeking major federal contracts, concessions, or control of assets in sectors designated by law as critical to national security or constitutional government operations to disclose their beneficial owners sufficiently to identify any foreign government, foreign national, foreign controlled entity, or organized criminal enterprise with a substantial interest or effective control. No such contract or concession may be awarded to an entity that cannot verify its beneficial ownership, or whose ownership is materially concealed through nominees, shell entities, or similar evasive structures. Law shall provide public transparency sufficient to enforce this Section. 1.14 — Prohibition on use of government resources for campaign purposes No federal official and no federal employee may use government staff, facilities, equipment, communications systems, or funds paid for by taxpayers for any activity whose primary purpose is to support or oppose a candidate, political party, or ballot measure. This prohibition applies to: (a) Directing government employees to perform campaign work during official hours (b) Using government offices, vehicles, aircraft, or equipment for campaign events or travel (c) Using official communications channels — email, social media accounts, letterhead, or mailing lists — to distribute campaign materials (d) Pressuring subordinate employees to donate to, volunteer for, or support any candidate or party Incidental, unavoidable overlap between official duties and public political activity — such as a President giving a speech that is also newsworthy — does not violate this Section. The test is whether the primary purpose of the activity is governmental or electoral. Violations shall be investigated and prosecuted by the Accountability Commission. A pattern of violations constitutes grounds for removal from office. Section 2 — Financial Market Integrity 2.1 — Purpose Financial markets are infrastructure of national importance. Fraud, manipulation, and insider trading in financial markets harm ordinary investors, undermine public trust, concentrate wealth illegitimately, and — at sufficient scale — threaten economic stability. The federal financial market regulator shall have the independence, funding, and enforcement powers necessary to deter and punish violations effectively. Toothless penalties and political interference in enforcement are incompatible with market integrity. 2.2 — Independence of the financial market regulator The primary federal financial market regulator established by Congress shall be structured as an independent regulatory agency within the meaning of Article XIII, with commissioners serving fixed terms and removable only for cause. Disagreement with regulatory decisions, enforcement priorities, or legal interpretations made in good faith is not cause. The President may not direct, delay, or terminate any specific enforcement action or investigation. 2.3 — Independent funding The financial market regulator shall be funded through fees assessed on regulated entities and market transactions, not through annual congressional appropriations. Congress may not use budget pressure to disable effective enforcement. 2.4 — Penalty floors Civil penalties for securities fraud, insider trading, and market manipulation shall be set at levels that actually deter. Congress shall establish a regime that includes: (a) Disgorgement of unlawful profit (b) Multiples of profit or loss avoided sufficient to deter (c) No nominal cap that would make serious fraud economically rational 2.5 — No settlement without admission for major fraud In major enforcement actions for securities fraud, market manipulation, or insider trading, settlement on a "neither admit nor deny" basis is prohibited. A defendant must either admit the conduct or proceed to adjudication. 2.6 — Criminal referral and prosecution When the financial market regulator determines that criminal conduct has occurred, it shall refer the matter promptly to the Accountability Commission for criminal prosecution. No financial institution shall be deemed too large, connected, or systemically important to prosecute. 2.7 — Additional Congressional mandates Congress shall provide the financial market regulator with authority to seek emergency judicial asset preservation where necessary to protect funds subject to disgorgement. Congress shall also provide for executive compensation clawback where compensation was based on fraudulent reporting or paid to officers responsible for serious violations, and shall maintain a whistleblower reward and protection program for financial market violations. 2.8 — Revolving door The financial market regulator is structurally vulnerable to capture through personnel rotation between the regulator and the financial industry. Both incoming and outgoing rotation must be governed by law. Congress shall establish: (a) Post service cooling off periods, graduated by seniority and access to confidential supervisory information, during which former personnel may not work for, represent, or advise regulated entities. The most senior positions shall carry the longest restrictions. (b) Pre service recusal for persons appointed from regulated entities, covering matters involving their former employer for a period set by law, and full disclosure of financial interests and contingent compensation at the time of appointment. Unvested or contingent arrangements that create an incentive to favor a former employer are void. (c) A prohibition on any regulated entity offering payment or financial benefit to a departing employee conditioned on that employee's future regulatory decisions or favorable treatment. Any such arrangement is void. (d) Transition support sufficient to ensure that post service restrictions do not function as a deterrent to qualified public service, and compensation during service adequate to attract qualified regulators. Law shall provide public transparency sufficient to enforce this Section. Violations by former officials are prosecuted by the Accountability Commission; violations by regulated institutions are subject to the civil penalty regime of Section 2.4. 2.9 — Functional jurisdiction and enforcement capacity The financial market regulator's jurisdiction extends to instruments that function as investments, securities, or derivatives regardless of technological form or evasive labeling. Congress shall not exempt an entire class of such instruments from effective oversight. Law shall provide investigative and surveillance authority sufficient to detect fraud, manipulation, and insider trading, subject to lawful use and constitutional constraint. Section 3 — The Civil Service 3.1 — Merit principle Federal employment in the executive branch shall be based on merit — qualifications, demonstrated competence, and performance. No person shall be hired, promoted, assigned, transferred, or removed from federal civil service employment on the basis of their political affiliation, political activity, or the political preferences of their appointing official, except for positions lawfully designated as policy making appointments. 3.2 — Protected positions The vast majority of federal employees occupy career civil service positions. These positions are protected from politically motivated removal. A career civil servant may be removed only for: (a) Demonstrated unsatisfactory performance following notice and an opportunity to improve (b) Misconduct (c) Elimination of the position for legitimate operational reasons, not as a pretext for political removal 3.3 — Policy appointments A defined number of senior positions in each agency may be designated as political appointments subject to the President's direction. Congress shall set the maximum number of such positions by law, subject to the constitutional cap established elsewhere in this Constitution. Political appointees serve at the pleasure of the appointing official. Reclassification of career positions beyond lawful limits is void. 3.4 — Whistleblower protections Federal employees who report to the Accountability Commission, to Congress, or to a supervisor evidence of: illegal conduct; waste, fraud, or abuse; violations of this Constitution; or dangers to public health or safety — are protected from retaliation. Retaliation against a federal whistleblower by any official is a criminal offense prosecutable by the Accountability Commission. 3.5 — Enforcement The Accountability Commission (Article XII) shall receive and investigate complaints of civil service violations, including politically motivated removals and retaliation against whistleblowers. Section 4 — Lobbying and Access 4.1 — Mandatory registration All persons who communicate with federal officials for the purpose of influencing legislation, regulation, or government decisions on behalf of another party for compensation shall register as lobbyists with the Accountability Commission. Registration shall include disclosure of clients, subject matters, and compensation received. 4.2 — Disclosure of contacts Lobbyists shall file periodic reports disclosing their contacts with federal officials, the subject matter of those contacts, and the positions advocated. Federal officials shall maintain and publish logs of substantive contacts with registered lobbyists. 4.3 — Gifts and hospitality No lobbyist may provide and no federal official may accept gifts, meals, travel, entertainment, or anything of value beyond a nominal threshold established by law. This prohibition applies regardless of whether the gift is connected to a specific official action. 4.4 — Revolving door — lobbyists to officials No registered lobbyist may assume a federal position with direct regulatory authority over matters on which they lobbied within the preceding three years, except upon a specific written waiver by the Accountability Commission finding no conflict of interest. 4.5 — Enforcement The Accountability Commission shall maintain the lobbyist registry, enforce disclosure requirements, and investigate violations. Violations are subject to civil penalties, criminal prosecution for willful violations, and deregistration. Section 5 — Enforcement 5.1 The provisions of this Article are enforced by the Accountability Commission (Article XII). The Commission's authority over the conduct covered by this Article — corruption, financial conflicts, civil service violations, and lobbying violations — cannot be overridden, transferred, or terminated by the President, Congress, or any other official. 5.2 The financial market regulator established under Section 2 of this Article operates as an independent enforcement body for civil market violations. Criminal referrals from that regulator are acted upon by the Accountability Commission under Section 2.6. Law shall provide coordination sufficient to prevent conflicting or duplicative proceedings over the same conduct. 5.3 Any federal official — including the President, any member of Congress, or any federal judge — is subject to investigation and prosecution under this Article. No office confers immunity from these provisions. 5.4 The Accountability Commission's authority to investigate and prosecute violations of this Article is part of the unamendable core of Article XI. It cannot be reduced, constrained, or eliminated by constitutional amendment. If the Accountability Commission becomes systemically captured or fails to perform its core functions for a prolonged period, the Supreme Court may declare it in institutional failure and trigger a reconstitution process established by law consistent with judicial independence and constitutional continuity.

docs.title: Article IX — Citizenship and National Membership
docs.summary: Establishes equal citizenship, national membership, domicile principles, overseas political participation, and constitutional rules for citizenship status. Citizenship is a legal status under this Constitution, but it is also a guarantee against hereditary caste, arbitrary exclusion, and unequal membership in the political community.

headings.text: Section 5 — Loss, Renunciation, and Conflict of Membership

docs.search_text: Article IX — Citizenship and National Membership Status: [ DRAFT ] Scope Establishes equal citizenship, national membership, domicile principles, overseas political participation, and constitutional rules for citizenship status. Citizenship is a legal status under this Constitution, but it is also a guarantee against hereditary caste, arbitrary exclusion, and unequal membership in the political community. Section 1 — Equal Citizenship 1.1 All citizens of the United States are equal in constitutional status. No distinction shall be drawn between citizens by birth and citizens by naturalization except where this Constitution expressly provides otherwise. 1.2 Citizenship carries equal protection of the laws, equal civic standing, and equal eligibility for public life subject only to qualifications expressly established by this Constitution. 1.3 No law may create ranks of citizenship, hereditary civic privilege, or permanent civic disability inconsistent with this Constitution. 1.4 Citizenship by birth or naturalization shall be defined by law consistent with this Constitution. No person born or naturalized under the laws of the United States may be denied equal constitutional standing once citizenship is lawfully recognized. 1.5 Congress shall establish by law a uniform rule of naturalization consistent with equal citizenship, due process, and this Constitution. Such law may distinguish among applicants by lawful residence, demonstrated ties, or other constitutionally sufficient criteria, but may not make naturalization permanently unavailable to any class of persons except on grounds consistent with this Constitution. Section 2 — Political Membership and Domicile 2.1 Citizenship is the foundation of national political membership under this Constitution, but voting, candidacy, and representation may be assigned by domicile, residence, or other constitutional criteria established by this Constitution or by law consistent with it. 2.2 Congress and the Electoral Commission shall provide uniform rules for domicile, residence, overseas voting assignment, and proof of citizenship sufficient to secure equal national membership without arbitrary exclusion. 2.3 No citizen may be denied federal electoral participation solely because that citizen resides abroad, was born abroad, or has changed domicile within the United States, except as this Constitution expressly permits. 2.4 No citizen may be assigned simultaneously to more than one state or federal electoral domicile for the same election. Congress and the Electoral Commission shall provide uniform rules to prevent duplication, manipulation, and disenfranchisement in domicile assignment. 2.5 A citizen who returns to reside in the United States resumes full political membership according to the domicile established upon return. Time spent abroad may affect electoral assignment only as provided by law consistent with this Article and may not permanently sever national political membership. Section 3 — Overseas Citizens 3.1 Every citizen of the United States residing outside the country retains the right to participate in federal elections. 3.2 Congress and the Electoral Commission may distinguish by law between short term and long term residence abroad for purposes of state assigned and federal only voting, provided that no citizen is wholly disenfranchised from federal elections by residence abroad. Military service, diplomatic service, and comparable public service abroad may not be treated as abandonment of national political membership. 3.3 Citizens who have never established domicile in a state shall be assigned by law to a constitutionally valid federal electoral home for participation in federal elections. That assignment may be tied to a citizen parent's last domicile, another lawful connecting principle, or a federal overseas pool, but may not leave the citizen without a practical federal electoral home. 3.4 Congress and the Electoral Commission shall provide secure systems for overseas registration, ballot delivery, and ballot return sufficient to make the right secured by this Section practical and timely. 3.5 Congress may distinguish among categories of overseas citizens only for purposes of electoral assignment, ballot administration, and security. No such distinction may wholly extinguish a citizen's participation in federal elections. Section 4 — Equal Eligibility for Office 4.1 Except as otherwise provided by this Constitution, naturalized citizens and citizens by birth are equally eligible for federal office. 4.2 Additional loyalty, sole citizenship, foreign service, or disclosure requirements for particular offices are valid only where this Constitution expressly provides for them or where they are narrowly tailored by law to offices involving exceptional national security responsibility. 4.3 No law may impose a blanket disability on citizens by naturalization from holding federal office. 4.4 Where another Article imposes office specific qualifications, incompatibilities, disclosure duties, or nationality restrictions, those requirements shall be read narrowly and shall not be expanded by implication into a general hierarchy of citizenship. 4.5 Office specific nationality, loyalty, or disclosure rules applicable to especially sensitive offices must be construed as exceptions to equal citizenship, not as a general warrant for ranked civic status. Section 5 — Loss, Renunciation, and Conflict of Membership 5.1 Congress may provide by law for voluntary renunciation of United States citizenship and for administrative procedures necessary to recognize that renunciation. 5.2 No citizen shall lose citizenship except by voluntary renunciation made knowingly and intentionally, or by a judicial finding that the person knowingly obtained citizenship by material fraud. Any proceeding to revoke citizenship must provide notice, a meaningful hearing before an independent tribunal, the right to counsel, and judicial review on the merits before revocation takes effect. No law may provide for involuntary loss of citizenship as punishment, political exclusion, or civil disability, or reduce the procedural protections required by this Section. 5.3 Congress may regulate dual citizenship, foreign allegiance, and foreign office incompatibility for holders of sensitive federal office, but may not impose civic disabilities on ordinary citizens solely for holding another nationality. 5.4 No citizen shall be rendered stateless by involuntary government action. Any law governing loss, renunciation, or suspension of citizenship status must be construed to avoid statelessness except where a citizen has voluntarily and knowingly renounced United States citizenship. Section 6 — Proof and Administration 6.1 Congress shall by law establish fair, uniform, and reviewable procedures for proving citizenship where citizenship is relevant to voting, officeholding, or other constitutional status. 6.2 No citizen may be stripped of political rights by administrative error without prompt notice, a meaningful opportunity to contest the determination, and judicial review. 6.3 No agency may impose documentary burdens, evidentiary presumptions, or administrative practices that make citizenship practically impossible to prove for ordinary eligible persons. 6.4 Judicial review under this Article shall be prompt where delay would impair voting, candidacy, officeholding, travel, or other time sensitive constitutional status. Section 7 — Construction 7.1 This Article shall be construed to preserve equal citizenship, practical political membership, and democratic inclusion. 7.2 Any ambiguity under this Article shall be resolved against hereditary privilege, caste like civic ranking, and arbitrary exclusion from national membership.

docs.title: Article X — Federalism and the States
docs.summary: Establishes the division of authority between the federal government and the states. The United States is a federal republic in which the national government and state governments each possess defined sovereign authority. Neither is a mere creation of the other. This Article replaces the prior Constitution's Articles IV and VI and the Tenth Amendment.

headings.text: Section 2 — Powers of the Federal Government
Section 3 — Powers Reserved to the States
Section 6 — Washington D.C. and the National Capital
Section 7 — Territories and Admission of New States
Section 10 — Tribal Nations and Indigenous Peoples

docs.search_text: Article X — Federalism and the States Status: [ DRAFT ] Scope Establishes the division of authority between the federal government and the states. The United States is a federal republic in which the national government and state governments each possess defined sovereign authority. Neither is a mere creation of the other. This Article replaces the prior Constitution's Articles IV and VI and the Tenth Amendment. Section 1 — Principles of Federalism 1.1 The powers of the federal government are those granted by this Constitution, expressly or by necessary implication. All other powers are reserved to the states or to the people. 1.2 State governments are sovereign within their reserved domains. The federal government may not commandeer state legislatures, state executives, or state courts to implement federal programs or enforce federal law against state will, except where this Constitution expressly provides otherwise. 1.3 The federal government and the states are partners in governance, not adversaries. Both are obligated to act in good faith toward the citizens they serve and toward each other. 1.4 Rights floor. The rights guaranteed by Article V are a minimum floor. States may enact laws that expand rights beyond the federal floor. No state may enact laws that reduce rights below the federal floor. Where a state law provides greater protection for a right than Article V requires, the state standard governs within that state. 1.5 Democratic floor. Every state shall maintain a republican form of government including free and fair elections, an independent judiciary, and separation of executive and legislative power. The federal government shall guarantee this form of government to every state and shall protect each state against invasion and, upon application of the state legislature or executive, against domestic violence. 1.5A — State judicial independence standards. The requirement of an independent judiciary in Section 1.5 includes the following minimum standards, which every state must satisfy. States may exceed but not fall below these floors. (a) Terms. Judges serving on courts of general jurisdiction shall serve terms of not less than six years. A state may provide for longer terms or life tenure but may not provide for shorter initial terms or at will judicial service. (b) Salary protection. The compensation of a state judge shall not be reduced during the judge's term of service. The legislature may increase but not decrease judicial compensation as a mechanism of influencing judicial decisions. (c) Removal. A state judge may be removed from office only: (i) by a process requiring a legislative supermajority equivalent to impeachment; (ii) upon reaching a mandatory retirement age established in advance by state law; (iii) upon a finding of misconduct by a judicial conduct commission as provided in subsection (d); or (iv) by recall election, only where the state uses elections for that judicial office and the same recall standards apply as for other elected officials. A judge may not be removed by simple legislative vote, executive order, or failure of reappointment taken in retaliation for judicial decisions. (d) Judicial conduct commission. Each state shall maintain an independent judicial conduct commission authorized to receive complaints, investigate misconduct, and impose discipline including censure, suspension, mandatory training, and referral for removal proceedings. No more than a minority of commission members may be sitting elected officials. Commission findings shall be made public. (e) Funding protection. State judicial branch funding shall not be reduced below the level necessary for courts to fulfill their constitutional functions. A legislature may not reduce judicial funding as a mechanism of pressure on judicial decisions. (f) No retaliation for decisions. No judicial officer shall be subjected to adverse action — including reassignment, pay reduction, or removal — based on the legal conclusions expressed in any decision, dissent, or opinion. The remedy for a disagreeable judicial decision is appeal; it is not discipline. 1.6 — Enforcement of the democratic floor. Where a state persistently and materially violates the democratic floor of Section 1.5, the following enforcement mechanism applies: (a) Finding. The Supreme Court, on petition by the United States or by the legislature or executive of any other state, may find that a state is in persistent and material violation of Section 1.5. The finding must identify the constitutional violations and explain why ordinary judicial relief has proved insufficient. (b) Congressional remedy. Upon a finding under Section 1.6(a), Congress shall within 180 days enact a remedial measure reasonably directed to restoring constitutional government in the state. Such measures may include federal administration of federal elections in the state, conditions on federal grants related to the violation, or other lawful means consistent with this Constitution. (c) Automatic interim remedy and mandatory suspension vote after remedial failure. If Congress fails to enact a remedial measure within the 180 day period of Section 1.6(b), federal elections conducted in the state shall immediately be administered under federally supervised procedures established by law and carried out through the Electoral Commission until Congress acts or the Supreme Court certifies compliance. During the same period, the House and the Regional Assembly shall each hold a recorded vote within the next 30 days on whether to suspend the state's representation until compliance is restored. Members from the affected state may not vote on that question, and the denominator shall be reduced accordingly. If either chamber fails to hold the required vote within that 30 day period, the state's representation in that chamber is automatically suspended as of the thirty first day, unless 2/3 of the full membership of that chamber has voted within the 30 day period to expressly decline suspension. The supermajority threshold ensures that automatic suspension reflects genuine legislative inaction rather than leadership or quorum obstruction, while making it meaningfully difficult to escape a consequence that follows a Supreme Court finding and a missed congressional remedy. (d) Suspension of representation. If the state remains in material violation one year after a Supreme Court finding under Section 1.6(a), the state's representation in the House and Regional Assembly is suspended automatically until the Supreme Court certifies compliance, unless Congress has already imposed suspension earlier under Section 1.6(c) or has enacted a remedial measure that the Supreme Court finds is producing substantial and timely progress toward compliance. Congress may extend that progress determination once for no more than 90 days by a 2/3 vote of both chambers. Suspension does not affect the constitutional rights of the state's residents, does not expel the state from the Union, and does not create a permanent blocking minority in the amendment process under Article XI. (e) Restoration. Representation shall be restored immediately upon Supreme Court certification that the state has returned to compliance with Section 1.5. 1.7 — Electoral integrity floor for state elections. Free and fair elections require, at minimum, that the identity of those funding candidates and campaigns be known to voters, that foreign money be excluded, and that the political spending of corporations and similar entities not be treated as equivalent to the political expression of citizens. These requirements are conditions of the democratic floor of Section 1.5. Every state shall maintain the following minimum standards for all elections conducted within its borders, including state and local elections: (a) Disclosure. All contributions and expenditures made for the purpose of influencing a state or local election shall be publicly disclosed. Disclosure shall identify the original human source of funds through all intermediaries. No entity may be used as a conduit to conceal the identity of the original human donor. A state may set stricter disclosure requirements than this floor but may not set weaker ones. (b) Anonymous contributions. No anonymous contribution above two hundred dollars may be made to any candidate, political committee, or entity making expenditures to influence a state or local election. States may lower but not raise this threshold. (c) Foreign money. No foreign national, foreign government, foreign corporation, or entity owned or controlled by foreign interests may contribute to or make expenditures in connection with any state or local election. This prohibition applies regardless of whether funds are routed through a domestic intermediary. (d) Corporate and organizational spending. Corporations, labor unions, trade associations, and other legal entities are not natural persons and do not possess the same constitutional rights as individual citizens with respect to political spending in state and local elections. States may regulate or prohibit their political expenditures without satisfying strict scrutiny. (e) Government contractors. No entity holding an active state or local government contract above a threshold set by state law may make political contributions or expenditures in elections for offices that exercise regulatory or contracting authority over that entity. States shall set the specific threshold and coverage by law but may not exempt state contractors from this principle entirely. (f) Cryptocurrency and digital assets. No contribution or expenditure to influence a state or local election may be made through a cryptocurrency or digital asset unless the full identity chain from the originating wallet to an identified natural person can be verified and disclosed in compliance with the disclosure requirement of Section 1.7(a). (g) Public financing option. Every state shall make available at least one public financing option for candidates for statewide office. The design, funding level, and matching structure are matters of state law; the obligation to provide an option is a condition of the democratic floor. (h) Enforcement. A state that persistently and materially fails to maintain the standards of this Section is in violation of the democratic floor of Section 1.5 and subject to the enforcement mechanism of Section 1.6. The Accountability Commission may refer evidence of systematic violations to the Supreme Court. 1.7A — Voting access floor for state elections. The right to vote is meaningless without meaningful access to the ballot. Every state shall maintain the following minimum access standards for all elections conducted within its borders, including state and local elections. These standards are conditions of the democratic floor of Section 1.5 and are enforceable through the mechanism of Section 1.6. (a) Right to vote in state elections. Every citizen eligible to vote in federal elections under Article I has the right to vote in state and local elections in the state of their residence. This right is subject to the same non discrimination requirements that govern federal elections. (b) Automatic registration. Each state shall automatically register eligible citizens to vote in state and local elections through the same administrative processes used for federal election registration under Article I. A citizen registered for federal elections in a state is automatically registered for that state's elections on the same roll. (c) Early voting. Each state shall provide at least ten days of early in person voting before any statewide election, including at least one weekend day. States may provide greater access. (d) No excuse absentee voting. Every eligible voter shall be permitted to vote by mail or absentee ballot without stating a reason. States may require a request in advance but may not limit mail voting to defined categories of excuse. (e) Identification requirements. A state may require voters to verify their identity. Any such requirement must: (i) accept a broad range of documents, including those obtainable without a fee; (ii) provide a free government issued credential to any voter who possesses no qualifying document; and (iii) not be implemented in a manner with a racially or otherwise discriminatorily disparate effect. An identification requirement shown to have such an effect is subject to strict scrutiny. (f) Restoration of voting rights. No state may deny the right to vote to any citizen who has completed their sentence of incarceration, including any period of parole or supervised release. No state may impose a waiting period, application fee, or discretionary approval process as a condition of restoring the franchise upon completion of sentence. (g) Enforcement. A state that persistently and materially fails to maintain the standards of this Section is in violation of the democratic floor of Section 1.5 and subject to the enforcement mechanism of Section 1.6. 1.8 — Federal incentive for full electoral integrity compliance. A state that voluntarily adopts campaign finance standards equivalent to or stricter than the full framework of Article VII — including outside spending caps, per actor aggregate limits, and the geographic concentration limit — shall be eligible to participate in the federal public financing matching infrastructure administered by the Electoral Commission. Participation allows state candidates to access federal matching fund infrastructure for qualifying small dollar contributions in state races, at the state's option and cost. This creates a positive incentive for states to go beyond the floor without requiring them to do so. 1.9 — Redistricting floor for state legislatures. The democratic floor of Section 1.5 includes neutral and reviewable districting for state legislative elections. Every state that elects members of its legislature from districts shall draw those districts under criteria that prohibit partisan or incumbent entrenchment, guarantee substantially equal population, protect racial and language minority voting rights, and require contiguity, compactness, and respect for political subdivisions except where deviation is necessary to satisfy superior constitutional requirements. Law shall provide public transparency, an opportunity for public comment, and expedited judicial review of state legislative maps before they take effect. Persistent and material violation of this Section is a violation of the democratic floor of Section 1.5. 1.10 — State emergency powers floor. No state governor or other state officer may use a state emergency declaration to suspend rights below the federal floor of Article V, to postpone a regularly scheduled state election except by order of the highest court of the state upon a written finding that a free and fair election on the scheduled date is impossible, or to continue emergency powers beyond 30 days without approval of the state legislature. No state emergency may remain in effect for more than 180 days in total without approval by 2/3 of the full membership of the state legislature. State law may impose stricter limits but not weaker ones. Section 2 — Powers of the Federal Government Congress shall have the power to: (a) Defense and security. Raise, maintain, and regulate the armed forces; declare war; provide for the common defense; establish rules for the conduct of warfare; enter into alliances and mutual defense treaties. (b) Foreign affairs. Conduct foreign policy; negotiate and ratify treaties; regulate relations with foreign governments and international organizations; define and punish offenses against the law of nations. (c) Commerce. Regulate commerce with foreign nations, among the states, and with Indian nations. This power includes the authority to regulate activities that substantially affect interstate commerce, establish rules for interstate transactions, and prevent states from erecting barriers to interstate trade. It does not include the power to compel individuals to engage in commerce. (d) Taxation and spending. Lay and collect taxes, duties, and imposts; borrow money on the credit of the United States; spend for the general welfare of the United States. Federal spending may attach conditions to grants to states, provided those conditions are clearly stated, directly related to the purpose of the grant, and not so coercive as to leave states no meaningful choice. (e) Currency and financial system. Coin money, regulate its value, and maintain the integrity of the monetary system; charter and regulate banks and financial institutions engaged in interstate activity; establish bankruptcy laws. (f) Immigration and naturalization. Establish uniform rules for immigration and the naturalization of citizens. States may not enact immigration enforcement regimes that conflict with federal immigration law, but may enact laws that extend benefits or protections to immigrants beyond the federal floor. (g) Civil rights. Enforce the rights guaranteed by this Constitution against infringement by state governments or private actors, through appropriate legislation. (h) Environment and natural resources. Regulate pollution, protect navigable waters, manage federal lands, and address environmental harms that cross state boundaries or affect the global commons. States may enact environmental standards stricter than federal standards. (i) Public health. Respond to public health emergencies that cross state lines; regulate the safety of food, drugs, and medical devices in interstate commerce; fund and coordinate national public health systems. (j) Infrastructure. Build and maintain infrastructure of national significance, including interstate highways, rail networks, air traffic systems, and communications infrastructure. (k) Science and knowledge. Fund scientific research; establish standards of weights and measures; grant patents and copyrights for limited terms to promote innovation and the useful arts. (l) Federal courts. Establish courts inferior to the Supreme Court, define their jurisdiction, and fund their operation. (m) Federal lands and territories. Exercise exclusive jurisdiction over federal lands, installations, and territories not admitted as states. (n) Postal and communications. Establish postal services and regulate interstate communications networks. (o) Necessary and proper. Make all laws necessary and proper for carrying into execution the foregoing powers and all other powers vested in the federal government by this Constitution. This power authorizes means reasonably adapted to enumerated ends; it does not create independent powers beyond those enumerated. The Necessary and Proper Clause may not be invoked to regulate activity that is (i) purely intrastate, (ii) not economic in nature, and (iii) has no substantial effect on interstate commerce; all three conditions must be present for this limitation to apply. Where invocation of the Clause would expand an enumerated power beyond its historical scope, courts shall apply heightened scrutiny, requiring the government to demonstrate a close and direct relationship between the means chosen and an enumerated end. Section 3 — Powers Reserved to the States 3.1 Powers not granted to the federal government by this Constitution, and not prohibited to the states, are reserved to the states or to the people. 3.2 The reserved powers of the states include, without limitation: (a) Criminal law — defining and punishing offenses against state law, operating police and corrections systems, subject to the rights guaranteed by Article V (b) Education — operating and funding public education above the federal floor established by Article XVIII, Section 2.2 (c) Land use and zoning — regulating the use of land within the state, including housing, development, and environmental conservation beyond federal minimums (d) Family law — regulating marriage, divorce, adoption, and child welfare, within the rights guaranteed by Article V (e) Professional licensing — establishing standards for occupations practiced within the state, subject to interstate recognition requirements of Section 5 (f) State taxation — levying taxes on residents and activity within the state, subject to the prohibition on burdening interstate commerce (g) Public health and safety — regulating health, safety, and welfare within the state, subject to federal preemption where applicable (h) Local government — creating, organizing, and empowering municipal governments, subject to the local government protections of Section 8 3.3 Laboratories of democracy. States are encouraged to experiment with innovative policies within their reserved domains. The federal government shall not preempt state policy experimentation except where the experiment creates demonstrable harm to residents of other states or violates the requirements of this Constitution. Where a state law causes demonstrable harm to residents of another state — including environmental contamination, financial fraud, or public health risks — the affected state has standing to sue the offending state in federal court without further jurisdictional prerequisites. Courts adjudicating such suits shall apply a rebuttable presumption that cross border harms of this nature are preemptable by the affected state's interests and by federal law; the offending state bears the burden of rebutting that presumption. Section 4 — Federal Preemption 4.1 Express preemption. Where Congress explicitly states that federal law preempts state law on a subject, federal law governs. Preemption clauses shall be construed narrowly — a statement that federal law governs a subject does not preempt state laws that supplement or exceed federal standards unless Congress explicitly says so. 4.2 Conflict preemption. Where compliance with both federal and state law is physically impossible, or where state law directly obstructs the achievement of a federal statutory purpose, federal law governs to the extent of the conflict. State law that merely exceeds federal standards does not conflict with federal law. 4.3 No implied field preemption. Federal regulation of a subject does not automatically preempt state regulation of the same subject. States may regulate concurrently with the federal government in most domains. Implied field preemption — the doctrine that federal regulation of an area implicitly occupies the entire field — is abolished except in the following domains where uniform national policy is constitutionally essential: (a) Foreign affairs and relations with foreign governments (b) National security and the conduct of warfare (c) Currency and monetary policy (d) Immigration status and naturalization 4.4 The concurrent regulation principle.
