The President may not commit the armed forces, cyber forces, intelligence personnel acting in a paramilitary capacity, or private military contractors in a combat role to any military operation abroad — offensive or defensive beyond immediate response — without either: (a) A formal declaration of war against a named state or state actor, passed by majority vote of both chambers of Congress; or (b) An Authorization for Use of Military Force (AUMF) meeting the requirements of Section 1.6 The distinction between "offensive" and "defensive" does not determine whether authorization is required. The duration, scale, and nature of the operation determine whether it constitutes a commitment of forces requiring authorization. A long running operation that began defensively requires authorization once it extends beyond the 30 day window of Section 1.5. 1.3 — Definition of Military Force. For purposes of this Article, "use of military force" includes: (a) Deployment of armed forces in a combat or combat support role in a foreign country (b) Offensive cyber operations — defined as operations designed to cause physical destruction, significant disruption to critical infrastructure, casualty producing effects, or the degradation of a foreign nation's military or governmental capacity (c) Lethal drone or aerial strikes against targets in a foreign country (d) Deployment of private military contractors in a combat role or in direct support of combat operations (e) Covert paramilitary operations conducted by intelligence agencies involving lethal force against foreign nationals (f) Naval blockades or maritime operations designed to coerce a foreign government Defensive cyber operations, intelligence collection operations that do not involve lethal force, sanctions, diplomatic action, and other non military countermeasures governed by Article XVII are not "use of military force" for purposes of this Article. 1.4 — Defensive Action Without Prior Authorization. The President may, without prior congressional authorization, order military action to: (a) Repel an attack on United States territory, United States armed forces, or treaty allies under active attack pursuant to Section 6 (b) Protect United States military forces under imminent and specific threat (c) Execute a time critical operation to rescue United States citizens in immediate physical danger (d) Respond to an offensive cyber attack against United States critical infrastructure by neutralizing the attacking capability, limited to disabling the specific systems, tools, or infrastructure used in the attack in progress Defensive action under this Section must be proportionate to the threat, limited in scope to the specific defensive objective, and reported to Congress under Section 1.5. It does not authorize a broader military campaign, occupation of foreign territory, or sustained hostilities. 1.5 — Reporting, Authorization, and Enforcement. (a) Reporting. Within 24 hours of ordering military action under Section 1.4 or any other use of military force, the President shall submit a written report to Congress describing the action, its legal basis, its anticipated duration, and the forces involved. (b) Authorization window. Military action taken under Section 1.4 must receive congressional authorization within 30 days or forces must be withdrawn. Failure of Congress to authorize within that period has the same legal effect as a vote against authorization and constitutes nonauthorization for every purpose of this Article. (c) Enforcement — withdrawal order. If authorization is not granted within 30 days, the Chief Justice of the Supreme Court, or the judicial officer designated under Article IV Section 2.7, shall issue a binding withdrawal order enforceable by the federal courts. The Secretary of Defense shall ensure compliance within the period fixed by law, which may not exceed 15 days. Presidential defiance of a withdrawal order is a grave constitutional violation and an absolute no immunity category under Article III Section 15. (d) Continued operations after withdrawal order. Ordering or maintaining military operations in violation of a withdrawal order is a violation of this Constitution, grounds for impeachment, a criminal abuse of official power, and an unlawful order under Section 8. Upon issuance of the withdrawal order, no funds may be obligated or expended for continued unlawful operations except as strictly necessary to protect forces during disengagement. If the President purports to continue the operation after the withdrawal order takes effect, lawful operational authority is limited to terminating, withdrawing, or defending withdrawing forces; no officer is required or permitted to continue the unlawful operation on the President's command. Upon the President's defiance of a withdrawal order, the Accountability Commission shall immediately issue a fiscal hold on appropriated funds for the unlawful operation. The hold is self executing and does not require congressional authorization or executive cooperation. The hold does not apply to expenditures strictly necessary to protect forces during withdrawal and disengagement, as preserved by the prior sentence; Congress shall by law establish the process by which the Accountability Commission identifies and designates such expenditures promptly upon issuing a hold. The Secretary of the Treasury shall implement the hold within 24 hours of the Commission's order. A Treasury officer who fails to implement a lawful fiscal hold within that period is subject to immediate removal from office and referral for prosecution by the Accountability Commission. The fiscal hold may be lifted only by the Chief Justice, or the judicial officer designated under Article IV Section 2.7, upon certification that the unlawful operation has ended and forces have been or are actively being withdrawn. (e) Expedited impeachment vote. A presidential order maintaining operations in violation of a withdrawal order shall trigger the expedited impeachment timetable of Article III Section 10.2A. (f) No self authorization. Only an act of Congress constitutes authorization under this Article. 1.6 — Requirements for an Authorization for Use of Military Force. An Authorization for Use of Military Force (AUMF) passed by Congress must: (a) Name the enemy specifically; open ended references to associated or future forces are void (b) State the objective of the authorization (c) Define the geographic scope (d) Define the categories of force authorized (e) Expire not more than two years after passage unless renewed by Congress (f) Require periodic reporting to Congress An AUMF that does not meet these requirements is void. Congress may not delegate open ended war making authority to the President through vague or unlimited authorizations. 1.7 — Sunset and Renewal of Existing AUMFs. All Authorizations for Use of Military Force enacted before ratification are void one year after ratification unless renewed in conformity with Section 1.6. The President shall report to Congress within 90 days on all operations then being conducted under pre existing authorizations. Section 2 — Cyber Warfare 2.1 — Offensive cyber operations require authorization. Offensive cyber operations as defined in Section 1.3(b) require the same congressional authorization as conventional military force. The President may not order an offensive cyber operation against a foreign nation or actor without a declaration of war, an AUMF covering that operation, or defensive authorization under Section 1.4(d). 2.2 — Proportionality. Offensive cyber operations must be proportionate to the threat or objective. Cyber operations designed to cause civilian casualties, destroy civilian infrastructure unrelated to military objectives, or produce effects equivalent to a conventional military attack require authorization commensurate with that level of force. Proportionality under this Section shall be judged by the scale of harm, the degree of civilian risk, and the relationship of the response to the triggering attack. 2.3 — Disclosure to Congress. All offensive cyber operations conducted under any authorization shall be disclosed to the relevant congressional oversight committees within 48 hours of execution. Classification of the specific operational details is permitted; the existence and general nature of the operation may not be withheld from oversight committees. 2.4 — Defensive cyber operations. The President may order defensive cyber operations — including active defense measures that operate on foreign systems to neutralize an ongoing attack — without prior congressional authorization. Defensive cyber operations must be reported to Congress within 24 hours under Section 1.5(a). Section 3 — Nuclear Weapons 3.1 — No unilateral first use. The President may not order the first use of nuclear weapons without confirmation by the Secretary of Defense and the Secretary of State that the order is lawful and warranted, a written finding by the Director of National Intelligence that no non nuclear response is sufficient, and immediate notification to congressional leadership. If either Secretary declines to confirm the order, it does not proceed. 3.2 — Congressional objection to first use. A first use order may not proceed if the Speaker of the House or the Majority Leader of the Regional Assembly formally objects, unless Congress overrides the objection by a 2/3 vote of both chambers. These requirements do not apply to a response to a confirmed nuclear attack governed by Section 3.3. 3.3 — Response to confirmed nuclear attack. If the United States or a treaty ally is under confirmed nuclear attack, the President may order a nuclear response without the confirmation requirement of Section 3.1. The response must be proportionate to the attack. Congressional leadership shall be notified as rapidly as the operational circumstances permit. 3.4 — Launch authority delegation. The President may not permanently or preemptively delegate nuclear launch authority to any other person. Temporary delegation during presidential incapacity follows the succession provisions of Article III Section 12. 3.5 — No autonomous launch. No automated system, artificial intelligence, or algorithm may be authorized to launch nuclear weapons without a direct human order from an authorized person. Autonomous or semi autonomous nuclear launch systems are prohibited. Section 4 — Private Military Contractors 4.1 — Authorization required. Private military contractors operating in a combat role or in direct support of combat operations abroad are subject to all war powers requirements of this Article. Their deployment must be reported to Congress under Section 1.5(a) as part of any military operation. They may not be used to circumvent the authorization requirements of Section 1.2. 4.2 — Laws of war. Private military contractors deployed by or on behalf of the United States government are subject to the laws of war and the Uniform Code of Military Justice. Violations are prosecutable in federal courts and by the Accountability Commission. 4.3 — Annual disclosure. The President shall report to Congress annually the number, locations, operational roles, and total cost of private military contractors deployed abroad. Operational details may be classified as necessary, but the existence, general location, mission category, and total cost of such deployments shall always be disclosed to the relevant congressional oversight committees. 4.4 — No substitution. Private military contractors may not be used as a substitute for authorized military force to avoid reporting requirements, to conduct operations in countries where military deployment would require authorization, or to conceal the scale of United States military engagement in a conflict. Section 5 — Covert Lethal Operations 5.1 — Authorization required. Covert operations conducted by intelligence agencies or special operations forces involving lethal force against foreign nationals require either: (a) An existing AUMF that covers the target, the territory, and the category of force; or (b) A lawful defensive justification under Section 1.4 together with prompt notification to the congressional intelligence leadership 5.2 — No operations against US citizens without warrant. No covert lethal operation may target a United States citizen anywhere in the world without a judicial warrant issued upon probable cause that the citizen poses an imminent and specific threat to the United States and that capture is not feasible. In exigent circumstances, a warrant application must be submitted within 72 hours after the operation. The warrant application and the court's ruling shall be made public within a period set by law unless a court makes a renewed finding that disclosure would cause specific and articulable harm to national security. 5.3 — Accountability Commission oversight. The Accountability Commission (Article XII) shall maintain a classified division with appropriate security clearances to audit covert lethal operations for compliance with this Section. The division shall report annually to the relevant congressional oversight committees in classified form and publish an unclassified summary. Section 6 — Treaty Based Defense Obligations A mutual defense treaty ratified by the Regional Assembly under Article III Section 9.2 constitutes advance congressional authorization for strictly defensive military action required to fulfill that specific treaty obligation. This authorization is limited to: (a) Defensive operations to repel an attack on the treaty ally (b) Operations specifically required by the terms of the ratified treaty (c) The scope and duration necessary to fulfill the immediate defensive obligation Any military action beyond strict treaty based defense — including offensive operations, operations in territories not covered by the treaty, or operations that extend beyond repelling the specific attack — requires separate congressional authorization under Section 1.2. The existence of a treaty obligation does not authorize an open ended military campaign. Section 7 — Domestic Deployment The armed forces, National Guard units in federal service, and all paramilitary forces under federal authority may not be deployed against civilians on domestic soil except: (a) To suppress an armed rebellion upon a declaration by Congress identifying the specific rebellion and authorizing specific military action (b) To provide disaster relief and humanitarian assistance, at the request of a state governor or by congressional authorization, in a non combat capacity (c) To protect specific federal facilities, personnel, or infrastructure against imminent physical attack, limited in scope to the specific protective mission For purposes of this Section, "armed rebellion" means the organized use of deadly force to overthrow government, seize and hold territory, or prevent federal law enforcement from operating in a defined area. Political protest, civil disobedience, and uncoordinated riots do not constitute armed rebellion under this Section. A presidential declaration of armed rebellion must be promptly reviewed by the Accountability Commission; if the Commission rejects the declaration, the deployment authority lapses. The President may not declare a domestic insurrection or rebellion for the purpose of deploying military force against peaceful protest, civil unrest, or political opposition. A deployment of armed forces against domestic civilians that is not authorized by this Section is void, and members of the armed forces are not required to carry it out under Section 8. Section 8 — Unlawful Orders No member of the armed forces, intelligence service, or any paramilitary force under federal authority may be ordered to carry out, and no such person is required to obey, a command that: (a) Violates this Constitution (b) Violates a law of the United States (c) Violates the laws of war as recognized under international treaty ratified by the United States (d) Orders the use of nuclear weapons in violation of Section 3 (e) Orders continued military operations in violation of a withdrawal order issued under Section 1.5(c) (f) Orders the deployment of armed forces against domestic civilians in violation of Section 7 A member of the armed forces who refuses to carry out an unlawful order under this Section is protected from punishment for that refusal. A superior who punishes a subordinate for refusing an unlawful order is subject to prosecution by the Accountability Commission. A member of the armed forces who carries out an unlawful order is not relieved of personal liability for the consequences of that order. Section 9 — Economic Security and Countermeasures Economic security and non military countermeasures are governed by Article XVII — Foreign Policy and National Security . Nothing in this Article enlarges military power by implication from those authorities. Section 10 — Foreign Influence Operations Foreign influence operations are governed by Article XVII — Foreign Policy and National Security and by Article VI where the conduct targets democratic processes directly. Section 11 — Critical Infrastructure Protection Critical infrastructure protection, outside the use of military force governed by this Article, is governed by Article XVII — Foreign Policy and National Security . Section 12 — Foreign Intelligence Collection Foreign intelligence collection is governed by Article XVII — Foreign Policy and National Security , subject to the limits on covert lethal operations established in Section 5 of this Article. Section 13 — Gray Zone Response Authority Gray zone response short of military force is governed by Article XVII — Foreign Policy and National Security . Measures that constitute military force remain subject to this Article. Section 14 — Alliance Reliability Alliance reliability, treaty execution duties, and non war alliance questions are governed by Article XVII — Foreign Policy and National Security , except where this Article governs the actual use of military force. Section 15 — Technology and Supply Chain Security Technology and supply chain security are governed by Article XVII — Foreign Policy and National Security .

docs.title: Article XVII — Foreign Policy and National Security
docs.summary: Establishes the constitutional framework for economic security, foreign influence defense, critical infrastructure protection, foreign intelligence collection, gray-zone response authority, alliance obligations, and technology security. These provisions address modern threats that fall below or alongside conventional military conflict, including economic coercion, information warfare, cyber attacks on civilian systems, and supply-chain vulnerabilities. Article XVI remains the exclusive framework for war, sustained hostilities, and other uses of military force.

headings.text: Section 7 — Technology and Supply Chain Security

docs.search_text: Article XVII — Foreign Policy and National Security Status: [ DRAFT ] Scope Establishes the constitutional framework for economic security, foreign influence defense, critical infrastructure protection, foreign intelligence collection, gray zone response authority, alliance obligations, and technology security. These provisions address modern threats that fall below or alongside conventional military conflict, including economic coercion, information warfare, cyber attacks on civilian systems, and supply chain vulnerabilities. Article XVI remains the exclusive framework for war, sustained hostilities, and other uses of military force. Section 1 — Economic Security and Countermeasures 1.1 — Presidential authority. The President may, in response to a foreign nation's or actor's actions that constitute a threat to the national security or economic security of the United States, impose the following economic countermeasures without prior congressional authorization: (a) Targeted sanctions against named foreign persons, entities, and government officials (b) Asset freezes against foreign government controlled entities operating in the United States (c) Restrictions on specific categories of exports to identified foreign adversaries (d) Emergency restrictions on foreign investment in critical sectors 1.2 — Congressional review. Economic countermeasures imposed under Section 1.1 shall be reported to Congress within 48 hours. Congress may by majority vote of both chambers terminate any countermeasure within 60 days of imposition. Countermeasures not terminated remain in effect until the President lifts them or Congress acts otherwise. 1.3 — National Security Investment Review. Congress shall establish a permanent National Security Investment Review body to screen foreign acquisitions of United States businesses, real property, and assets in sectors critical to national security. The Review body shall operate independently of political direction. Its decisions to block or condition acquisitions shall be based on objective national security criteria established by law. Any presidential override of a blocking decision must be made in writing, transmitted promptly to Congress, and remains subject to nullification by law. 1.4 — Critical sectors defined. Congress shall define by law the sectors subject to investment review under Section 1.3. At minimum, the following shall be designated critical: defense and aerospace; semiconductors and advanced computing; telecommunications infrastructure; artificial intelligence; biotechnology and pharmaceuticals; energy production and distribution; water systems; and financial market infrastructure. 1.5 — Economic coercion as national security threat. Systematic foreign government efforts to create economic dependence — including through state subsidized market manipulation, predatory investment, and infrastructure financing designed to establish leverage — shall be recognized as a national security threat. The President shall report to Congress annually on identified economic coercion campaigns and the countermeasures taken or proposed. Section 2 — Foreign Influence Operations 2.1 — Scope. Foreign adversary influence operations extend beyond election interference to include ongoing campaigns to shape domestic political opinion, policy, and institutions through covert or undisclosed foreign government direction. This Section addresses those operations without limiting protected speech or legitimate foreign engagement. 2.2 — Mandatory disclosure of foreign government funding. Any organization operating within the United States — including nonprofit organizations, think tanks, academic institutions, media organizations, cultural programs, and advocacy groups — that receives funding from a foreign government or a foreign government controlled entity shall register with the Accountability Commission and publicly disclose: (a) The identity of the foreign government or entity providing funds (b) The amount received, updated quarterly (c) The purposes for which the funds are used This requirement applies regardless of whether the organization engages in political activity. Disclosure is required for any foreign government funding above a threshold set by Congress. The constitutional floor for foreign government funding registration is $10,000 per year; Congress may set lower thresholds but not higher. Non disclosure is a criminal offense. The Accountability Commission is the enforcement authority. 2.3 — State sponsored foreign media. Any media organization that is editorially directed, substantially funded, or operationally controlled by a foreign government shall be registered as a foreign government media outlet with the Accountability Commission. All content produced by a registered foreign government media outlet distributed within the United States shall be labeled as such. Registration does not prohibit distribution — it requires transparency. Failure to register is a criminal offense. In addition to mandatory registration, state sponsored media must display a prominent, clear, and standardized disclosure label on all content; the label must identify the sponsoring government and the relationship between the media outlet and that government; platforms that host state sponsored media are required to enforce the labeling requirement. 2.4 — Academic and research institutions. Academic and research institutions receiving foreign government funding above a threshold set by Congress for research programs, academic chairs, or institutional programs shall disclose that funding publicly. Institutions may not accept foreign government funding subject to conditions that restrict research conclusions, limit publication, or require access to research results by the foreign government. Accepting such conditions voids the institution's eligibility for federal funding. 2.5 — Enforcement. The Accountability Commission shall maintain a public registry of all organizations registered under this Section. The Commission shall investigate suspected undisclosed foreign government funding operations and refer criminal violations for prosecution. The Commission shall publish an annual report on identified foreign influence operations and their sponsors. Section 3 — Critical Infrastructure Protection 3.1 — Federal responsibility. The federal government bears primary responsibility for protecting critical infrastructure — as defined in Section 1.4 of this Article — from attack, sabotage, and degradation by foreign governments, foreign controlled entities, and terrorist organizations. This responsibility cannot be fully delegated to private owners of critical infrastructure. 3.2 — Minimum security standards. Congress shall establish by law minimum security standards for all critical infrastructure, whether publicly or privately owned. The standards shall address physical security, cybersecurity, supply chain integrity, and resilience to attack or disruption. Owners and operators of critical infrastructure shall comply with these standards as a condition of operating in the United States. Congress shall review and update these standards not less than every five years. 3.3 — Federal assessment authority. The executive branch shall conduct regular security assessments of all designated critical infrastructure. Owners and operators of critical infrastructure shall cooperate with federal security assessments. The discovery of known foreign government implanted malware, surveillance tools, or access mechanisms in critical infrastructure systems shall be reported promptly to Congress. 3.4 — Foreign ownership of critical infrastructure prohibited. No foreign government, foreign government controlled entity, or entity with a foreign government beneficial owner holding more than a threshold set by Congress may own, operate, or hold a controlling interest in any designated critical infrastructure within the United States. Existing holdings in violation of this Section shall be divested within a period set by law that may not exceed two years after ratification or designation. 3.5 — Attack on critical infrastructure as act of war. A foreign government directed attack on United States critical infrastructure that causes significant physical damage, significant disruption to essential services, or loss of life shall be treated as an act of war for purposes of Article XVI. Section 4 — Foreign Intelligence Collection 4.1 — Purpose and balance. The collection of foreign intelligence — information about the activities, intentions, and capabilities of foreign governments, foreign organizations, and foreign nationals — is a necessary function of national security. This Section establishes a constitutional framework for foreign intelligence collection that preserves civil liberties while enabling effective defense against foreign threats. 4.2 — Foreign Intelligence Court. Congress shall establish a specialized Foreign Intelligence Court composed of federal judges serving renewable three year assignments, drawn from the Article IV judiciary. The Court shall: (a) Issue warrants for the collection of foreign intelligence within the United States (b) Review the legality and scope of intelligence collection programs (c) Publish annual statistics on warrants issued, denied, and modified, in unclassified form (d) Appoint independent counsel to argue against government warrant applications in significant cases 4.3 — Warrant requirements for collection. Collection of foreign intelligence that involves the interception of communications, the search of premises, or the collection of data about specific identified persons within the United States requires a warrant from the Foreign Intelligence Court upon a showing of: (a) Probable cause that the target is an agent of a foreign power or a foreign intelligence service (b) That the information sought is foreign intelligence information (c) That the collection will be conducted using minimization procedures that limit the retention and dissemination of information about United States persons "Agent of a foreign power" means a person who (a) acts at the direction or control of a foreign government or intelligence service, (b) engages in clandestine intelligence activities on behalf of a foreign power, or (c) knowingly engages in sabotage, terrorism, or the unauthorized gathering of defense information for a foreign power; journalists, academics, and advocates engaged in lawful activity do not qualify regardless of foreign contact. 4.4 — United States persons — heightened protection. Collection of foreign intelligence that targets a United States citizen or permanent resident requires a higher showing: probable cause that the person is knowingly engaged in clandestine intelligence activities, sabotage, or terrorism on behalf of a foreign power. Incidental collection of United States persons' communications during foreign targeted collection shall be minimized; communications of United States persons may not be searched without a separate warrant under Section 4.3. 4.5 — Bulk collection limitations. Bulk collection of communications is permitted only for foreign to foreign communications transiting United States infrastructure. Bulk collection of domestic communications, or of communications where one end is a United States person, is prohibited without individual warrants under Section 4.3. 4.6 — Congressional oversight. All foreign intelligence collection programs shall be disclosed to the relevant congressional oversight committees. No collection program whose existence is concealed from Congress is lawful. The Accountability Commission's classified oversight division under Article XII Section 3 shall audit compliance with this Section annually. 4.7 — No domestic political use. Foreign intelligence collection authorities may not be used to collect information on domestic political organizations, candidates, elected officials, journalists, or citizens on the basis of their political views or associations. Use of foreign intelligence authorities for domestic political surveillance is a criminal offense prosecutable by the Accountability Commission without a statute of limitations. Section 5 — Gray Zone Response Authority 5.1 — Definition. Gray zone operations are hostile actions by foreign states or state directed actors that fall below the threshold of conventional military attack but are designed to harm United States interests, undermine democratic institutions, or coerce United States policy. They include: state sponsored disinformation campaigns targeting domestic political processes; foreign government directed sabotage of infrastructure; economic coercion through market manipulation or weaponized financial leverage; and hybrid operations combining cyber, information, and proxy forces. 5.2 — Presidential response authority. The President may respond proportionately to identified gray zone operations without triggering Article XVI only where the response itself does not constitute a use of military force: (a) The President must determine in writing that the operation constitutes a gray zone attack by an identified foreign state or state directed actor (b) The response must be proportionate to the nature and scale of the gray zone operation (c) Congressional notification must occur before gray zone operations begin unless delay would cause immediate and serious harm, in which case notification must occur promptly thereafter 5.3 — Scope of gray zone response. Permissible responses to gray zone operations include counter disinformation measures, economic countermeasures under Section 1, cyber responses that do not amount to military force under Article XVI, diplomatic measures, and targeted sanctions. Gray zone authority does not authorize sustained hostilities, territorial occupation, lethal operations beyond Article XVI, or any other measure that constitutes military force. 5.4 — Attribution standard. A gray zone determination under Section 5.2 requires a written intelligence assessment attributing the operation to a foreign state or state directed actor with high confidence. The determination must be reviewed by the Director of National Intelligence and the Attorney General and provided to the congressional intelligence committees. Section 6 — Alliance Reliability 6.1 — Treaty commitments are binding. The President shall faithfully execute all mutual defense and collective security treaty obligations ratified by the Regional Assembly. The President may not selectively honor, condition, or withhold treaty obligations based on personal, political, financial, or extraneous considerations. 6.2 — No conditioning of alliance obligations. A President who conditions the fulfillment of a treaty alliance commitment — including military assistance, intelligence sharing, or collective defense obligations — on any consideration other than the treaty's terms and the requirements of this Constitution violates the oath of office and is subject to prosecution by the Accountability Commission under Article VI Section 1 and to impeachment under Article III Section 10. 6.3 — Congressional affirmation. Either chamber of Congress may by resolution independently affirm the United States' commitment to any treaty alliance. Such a resolution does not bind the President to take specific military action but creates a public record of congressional intent and may be considered in any proceeding under Section 6.2. 6.4 — Withdrawal from collective security treaties. The President may not withdraw the United States from any mutual defense or collective security treaty without the approval of a 2/3 vote of the Regional Assembly. The 2/3 requirement for collective security treaties is higher than the majority required for other treaty withdrawal under Article III Section 9.4, reflecting the particular destabilizing effect of withdrawing from collective defense arrangements. Section 7 — Technology and Supply Chain Security 7.1 — Strategic technology protection. The federal government has a responsibility to prevent foreign adversaries from acquiring, through investment, theft, academic transfer, or market manipulation, technology that would provide them a decisive military, intelligence, or economic advantage over the United States. 7.2 — Export controls. Congress shall maintain a system of export controls on technologies with significant national security implications. The executive branch shall administer export controls through an independent body whose decisions are based on objective security criteria. Export control decisions shall be reviewed annually and updated to reflect technological developments. Allies and partners may be granted preferential treatment in export licensing consistent with treaty obligations. 7.3 — Technology transfer through academic and research institutions. The executive branch shall maintain a program to identify and mitigate foreign adversary efforts to acquire sensitive technology through academic partnerships, research collaborations, and talent recruitment programs. Federal funding for research in sensitive technology areas shall be conditioned on recipient institutions maintaining appropriate controls on foreign participation. This Section shall be implemented in a manner that does not restrict legitimate international scientific collaboration or discriminate against researchers on the basis of national origin. 7.4 — Supply chain security. The federal government shall assess and mitigate national security risks in the supply chains for critical technology, defense systems, and critical infrastructure. Congress shall by law establish requirements for supply chain security assessment as a condition of federal procurement. Equipment from foreign adversary manufacturers shall not be used in federal systems or designated critical infrastructure where the Accountability Commission's technical review finds a material security risk. 7.5 — No targeting of persons by national origin. Nothing in this Article authorizes surveillance, investigation, restriction of employment, or any adverse action against any person solely on the basis of national origin, ethnicity, or country of birth. Technology security measures must be based on specific and articulable security concerns, not on demographic characteristics. Racial or ethnic profiling in the implementation of this Article is prohibited.

docs.title: Article XVIII — Social, Economic, and Affirmative Rights
docs.summary: Establishes social, economic, environmental, and affirmative rights of all persons within the United States. These rights impose positive duties on government to maintain constitutional floors of protection against destitution, exclusion, and preventable harm inconsistent with a minimally dignified life. They complement the civil and political rights in Article V. The general principles of Article V Section 1, including the rights floor, the prohibition on permanent suspension, absolute rights, and the rule that enumeration does not deny unenumerated rights, apply equally to the rights in this Article. Judicial enforcement under this Article is limited to deciding whether government has met the constitutional floor, whether government has impermissibly backslid from that floor, and what timely lawful remedy is required. Courts may not govern program design in the place of the political branches.
docs.search_text: Article XVIII — Social, Economic, and Affirmative Rights Status: [ DRAFT ] Scope Establishes social, economic, environmental, and affirmative rights of all persons within the United States. These rights impose positive duties on government to maintain constitutional floors of protection against destitution, exclusion, and preventable harm inconsistent with a minimally dignified life. They complement the civil and political rights in Article V. The general principles of Article V Section 1, including the rights floor, the prohibition on permanent suspension, absolute rights, and the rule that enumeration does not deny unenumerated rights, apply equally to the rights in this Article. Judicial enforcement under this Article is limited to deciding whether government has met the constitutional floor, whether government has impermissibly backslid from that floor, and what timely lawful remedy is required. Courts may not govern program design in the place of the political branches. Section 1 — Environmental Rights 1.1 Every person has the right to a healthy environment, including clean air, safe drinking water, and freedom from toxic contamination. 1.2 Every person has the right to a stable climate. The government has a duty to adopt and maintain reasonable measures, consistent with the best available scientific evidence, to prevent dangerous interference with the climate system and to protect present and future generations from the consequences of climate change. Government may not knowingly and materially dismantle existing climate protections without replacing them with measures that are constitutionally sufficient in aggregate. A court finding a violation of this duty may declare the breach, require the political branches to adopt a constitutionally sufficient remedial plan within a defined period, and retain jurisdiction to determine whether the submitted plan meets the constitutional floor. A court may not prescribe emissions targets, technology choices, tax measures, or line item appropriations. 1.3 In all decisions affecting the environment, the government shall give weight to the interests of future generations who cannot participate in current political processes. 1.4 The rights in this Section create enforceable duties on government. A court may determine whether government action or inaction is plainly inadequate to the constitutional floor established by this Section, may declare a violation, may require the submission of a remedial plan within a defined period, and may enforce compliance against executive officials charged with carrying out that plan. Courts shall not prescribe specific policy instruments, manage ongoing environmental programs, or set budget levels except where necessary to prevent the executive from disregarding an existing lawful appropriation. Section 2 — Affirmative Rights 2.1 Healthcare. Every person has the right to access basic healthcare and emergency medical treatment. No person shall be denied emergency medical treatment due to inability to pay. Congress shall establish and maintain a system that guarantees universal access to medically necessary care and under which inability to pay does not bar such access. Congress shall regulate public and private healthcare financing and delivery to prevent exclusion, arbitrary denial of medically necessary care, and materially abusive practices inconsistent with the right secured by this Section. This duty may be fulfilled through public provision, public insurance, regulated private participation, or a mixed system, but private participation does not diminish the government's constitutional obligation to secure the right. 2.2 Education. Every person has the right to free public education through the completion of secondary school. Public education shall be of sufficient quality to enable meaningful participation in civic and economic life. No state may provide substantially inferior education to any group of students on the basis of race, income, disability, immigration status, or geographic location. 2.3 Housing security. Every person has the right to protection against arbitrary homelessness. Government shall maintain measures sufficient to prevent destitution and to preserve access to basic shelter for persons who would otherwise be left without it through circumstances beyond their control. This right does not guarantee any specific dwelling, but it does require a constitutionally adequate shelter and homelessness prevention floor. 2.4 Enforcement of affirmative rights. The rights in this Section impose duties on government that are judicially enforceable. A court finding a violation may declare that the constitutional floor has not been met, require Congress or the relevant state legislature to adopt a lawful remedy within a defined period, order the executive to use existing lawful authority in the interim, and retain jurisdiction to determine whether the adopted remedy is constitutionally sufficient. Courts may not design a program, choose among materially sufficient policy alternatives, order tax increases, or compel new appropriations as a primary remedy. Government may not deliberately dismantle an existing system of protection below the constitutional floor unless it simultaneously replaces it with protections that are materially equal or greater in practical effect. If the political branches fail to act in good faith by the court's deadline, the court may preserve existing protections against backsliding and enforce compliance orders against responsible executive officials. Section 3 — Economic Rights 3.1 Right to work. Every person has the right to work and to pursue a lawful occupation free from arbitrary government restriction. Licensing requirements for occupations must be rationally related to the protection of public health, safety, or welfare. 3.2 Right to organize. Every worker has the right to form, join, and be represented by a labor union or other collective bargaining organization, and to engage in collective bargaining. No law shall prohibit collective bargaining or penalize workers for organizing. 3.3 Economic security. Congress shall maintain programs ensuring a basic standard of living for persons unable to support themselves due to age, disability, illness, caregiving burden, or economic hardship. Such protections may not be eliminated or materially reduced without replacement by protections that are constitutionally sufficient and materially equal or greater in practical effect. 3.4 Fair compensation. Every worker has the right to a living wage: compensation sufficient to meet basic needs including food, housing, healthcare, and transportation in the region where they work. Congress shall establish a minimum wage floor meeting this standard, set the specific amount by law, and review and adjust it at regular intervals sufficient to prevent erosion below the constitutional floor. States may set higher minimums. Congress may not freeze, delay, or reduce the wage floor below what this Section requires. Section 4 — Rights of Children 4.1 — Children as rights holders. Children are rights holders under this Constitution. The rights enumerated in this Article and in Article V apply to children as well as adults. Where rights require modification in their application to children by reason of age and developing capacity, such modification shall be made in the child's best interests, not for the convenience of the state or other adults. 4.2 — Right to education. Every child has the right to free public education of sufficient quality to develop their intellectual and civic capacities. No child may be denied education on the basis of their race, national origin, disability, immigration status, gender, or the circumstances of their parents. 4.3 — Protection from abuse, neglect, and exploitation. Every child has the right to protection from physical abuse, sexual abuse, emotional abuse, neglect, and economic exploitation. The state has an affirmative duty to protect children from these harms, including harms inflicted by parents, guardians, and institutions. 4.4 — Protection from child labor. No child under sixteen years of age may be employed in work that interferes with their education, endangers their health, or is hazardous in nature. Congress shall define and enforce standards protecting children from exploitative labor. Light, non hazardous work by older children that does not interfere with education is not prohibited. 4.5 — Right to be heard. In any legal proceeding that directly affects a child's welfare — including custody, adoption, guardianship, and child protective proceedings — the child's views shall be given weight appropriate to the child's age and maturity. A child has the right to independent representation in any proceeding in which custody, placement, or comparable fundamental interests are at issue. Where a court departs materially from a mature child's expressed preference, it shall state specific reasons on the record grounded in the child's welfare. 4.6 — Best interests standard. In all actions by government affecting children, the best interests of the child shall be a primary consideration. This standard applies to legislative, executive, and judicial action and may not be displaced by administrative convenience, blanket stigma, or categorical assumptions about family status. 4.7 — Children of incarcerated parents. The incarceration of a parent does not, by itself, constitute grounds for the permanent termination of parental rights. The state shall make reasonable efforts to maintain family bonds where doing so is in the child's best interests.

docs.title: Article XIX — Ratification and Transition
docs.summary: Governs ratification, legal continuity, supersession of the prior constitutional order, first-cycle implementation, and transition to the institutions established by this Constitution. This Article is read to favor prompt, lawful, and democratically legitimate establishment of the new constitutional order.

headings.text: Section 3 — Carried-Forward and Superseded Constitutional Provisions

docs.search_text: Article XIX — Ratification and Transition Status: [ DRAFT ] Scope Governs ratification, legal continuity, supersession of the prior constitutional order, first cycle implementation, and transition to the institutions established by this Constitution. This Article is read to favor prompt, lawful, and democratically legitimate establishment of the new constitutional order. Section 1 — Ratification 1.1 This Constitution shall take effect upon ratification by conventions or legislatures in three fourths of the states, as Congress shall determine by law. 1.2 Ratification under this Section supersedes the prior Constitution of the United States except as expressly carried forward by this Article or by other provisions of this Constitution. 1.3 Upon ratification, all federal and state officers shall remain bound to preserve, protect, and defend this Constitution in place of the prior Constitution. Section 2 — Continuity of Law 2.1 All federal statutes, treaties, regulations, judicial judgments, and lawful obligations in force at the time of ratification remain in force unless they conflict with this Constitution, are superseded by laws enacted under it, or expire by their own terms. 2.2 Existing offices, departments, courts, and agencies continue temporarily insofar as necessary to preserve legal continuity, but must be conformed to this Constitution within the periods provided by law or by this Article. 2.3 No existing law shall be given effect after ratification in a manner inconsistent with any right, structural rule, or institutional limitation established by this Constitution. Section 3 — Carried Forward and Superseded Constitutional Provisions 3.1 Rights protections from the prior Constitution that are consistent with this Constitution are carried forward only to the extent incorporated into Article V or otherwise restated here. 3.2 All prior constitutional provisions governing the structure, powers, composition, selection, terms, succession, or procedures of the federal branches are superseded by Articles I through IV and Articles VII through XVIII of this Constitution. 3.3 All prior constitutional provisions inconsistent with direct national election of the President, the abolition of the Electoral College, the Regional Assembly, fixed Supreme Court terms, the Constitutional Organs, or the amendment framework of this Constitution are superseded. 3.4 Congress shall by law publish an official transition schedule identifying the principal prior constitutional provisions carried forward, superseded, or restated, but the legal effect of this Constitution does not depend on that publication. 3.5 No officer, court, or agency may use uncertainty about transition to preserve a superseded constitutional mechanism once this Constitution has made the contrary rule effective by its own terms. Section 4 — First Elections and Terms 4.1 Congress shall by law provide for the first federal elections held under this Constitution and for the orderly commencement of terms under the new constitutional order. 4.2 The first elections under this Constitution must be conducted no later than one year after ratification unless this Constitution expressly provides otherwise. 4.3 Holders of offices abolished or materially transformed by this Constitution shall continue temporarily only for the time strictly necessary to transfer authority to the offices created by this Constitution. 4.4 Congress shall by law provide initial staggering, temporary seat allocation, and transitional succession arrangements necessary to establish the House of Representatives, the Regional Assembly, and the new election system, but no such law may alter the permanent constitutional design. 4.5 If Congress fails to provide by law for a first federal election on the timetable required by this Constitution, the Electoral Commission shall administer the election under interim rules consistent with this Constitution and subject to expedited judicial review. Section 5 — Judicial and Institutional Transition 5.1 Congress shall by law provide for the transition from the prior federal judiciary to the judicial structure established by Article IV, including the sequencing of terms, temporary service, the commencement of the Article IV appointment schedule, the constitution of the Judicial Nominations Commission, and the establishment of lower court terms consistent with that Article. 5.2 The Constitutional Organs established by Article XII shall be constituted as soon as practicable after ratification and shall assume their constitutional functions on the timeline provided by law, but no later than one year after ratification unless impossible due to conditions identified publicly by the Supreme Court. 5.2A If the deadline in Section 5.2 is missed and no impossibility finding has been identified publicly by the Supreme Court, the Court shall order completion of the applicable appointment process within 30 days. If that period also expires without constitution of the affected Organ, a majority of the Supreme Court may appoint temporary Commissioners meeting the qualifications of Article XII in the number strictly necessary to create a lawful quorum and permit the Organ to begin performing its startup, continuity, and protective functions. Temporary Commissioners may exercise only those powers necessary to begin lawful operation and preserve constitutional continuity, and their appointments expire when the ordinary appointment process is completed or after 180 days, whichever comes first, unless renewed once by written judicial findings that completion remains unlawfully delayed despite good faith efforts. 5.3 Existing agencies and officers whose functions are reassigned under this Constitution shall continue only until those functions are lawfully transferred, replaced, or terminated consistent with this Constitution. 5.4 Congress shall by law establish the initial terms, appointments, and temporary staffing needed to bring Articles XII through XV into operation without interrupting the lawful functioning of the government. Section 6 — Electoral and Executive Transition 6.1 The lawful constitutional transfer of power to officers elected or confirmed under this Constitution is mandatory. No outgoing official may obstruct the establishment of the constitutional order or use the powers of an expiring office to frustrate transition. Branch specific transfer duties remain applicable where separately stated in this Constitution. 6.2 Congress shall provide public funding, access, records, security, and administrative support sufficient for constitutional transition. 6.3 Obstruction of the ratified constitutional transition through force, fraud, abuse of official authority, or deliberate refusal to transfer lawful power is anti subversion and grounds for removal, disqualification, and prosecution. 6.4 No outgoing officer may use emergency authority, appointment power, classified control, fiscal control, or control of records to frustrate the lawful implementation of this Constitution after ratification. 6.5 No outgoing officer or federal employee may deliberately destroy, alter, conceal, or transfer outside lawful custody any federal record, communication, or official document during a constitutional transition. Deliberate destruction or concealment of public records during transition is anti subversion conduct under Article VI Section 1.1 and grounds for prosecution by the Accountability Commission. This provision applies to all federal records and is independent of the protections for electoral records in Article VI Section 1.2(c). Section 7 — Temporary Corrective Legislation 7.1 For ten years after ratification, Congress may enact corrective legislation under this Section only to address a genuine procedural gap, a direct textual conflict, or a missing sequencing rule arising from implementation of this Constitution. Corrective legislation may not expand any power, restrict any right, modify any structural provision beyond the identified defect, or address matters left to ordinary legislation. Each corrective act must identify with specificity the constitutional defect it addresses. The authority of this Section expires ten years after ratification and may not be extended. 7.2 The Supreme Court shall have expedited jurisdiction to review any claim that purported corrective legislation exceeds the authority of this Section. Any ambiguity about whether an act qualifies under this Section shall be resolved against the legislation. 7.3 Corrective legislation under this Section may not be used to preserve a superseded office, re create a superseded veto point, postpone a right, election, or institutional safeguard whose effective date is fixed by this Constitution, or fill any gap that this Constitution itself addresses through a self executing default rule. Section 8 — First Amendment Cycle 8.1 No Track 1 structural amendment may be proposed until ten years after ratification except to correct technical errors or conflicts identified during implementation. This cooling off period allows the structural constitutional order to stabilize before revision begins. 8.2 Track 2 rights amendments remain permitted immediately on the timetable established by Article XI. Rights expansion is not frozen during the structural cooling off period. 8.3 The amendment rules of Article XI shall be read in harmony with this Article during the first federal cycle under this Constitution. 8.4 During the first federal cycle under this Constitution, courts shall prefer interpretations that preserve lawful continuity without diluting rights, democratic accountability, or anti subversion safeguards expressly established by this Constitution. Section 9 — Construction 9.1 This Article shall be construed to favor legal continuity, democratic legitimacy, and the prompt establishment of the constitutional order created by this Constitution. 9.2 No ambiguity in transition shall be resolved in favor of preserving obsolete institutions, delaying constitutional implementation, or frustrating the lawful transfer of authority.

docs.title: Constitution Index
docs.summary: This is the master index for the revised Constitution. Each major component lives in its own file for easier editing and review. This document tracks the status of each part and provides navigation.
docs.search_text: Constitution of the United States of America Revised Draft — Working Document Status: In Progress Last Updated: 2026 03 28 Version: 0.1 (Scaffold) How This Document Works This is the master index for the revised Constitution. Each major component lives in its own file for easier editing and review. This document tracks the status of each part and provides navigation. Status Legend [ OUTLINE ] — structure defined, not yet drafted [ DRAFT ] — text written, under review [ REVIEW ] — being actively revised [ FINAL ] — agreed and locked Document Structure Preamble | File | Status | Notes | | | | | | preamble.md | [ DRAFT ] | Statement of purpose and founding principles | Articles | | Title | File | Status | Notes | | | | | | | | I | The Electoral System | articles/I electoral system.md | [ DRAFT ] | STV, multi member districts, voter rights, citizens' initiative, redistricting | | II | The Legislature | articles/II legislature.md | [ DRAFT ] | House (500 seats, STV), Regional Assembly (8 regions), asymmetric powers, deadlock resolution, and legislative control over public finance with substantive fiscal rules now housed in Articles XIV and XV | | III | The Executive | articles/III executive.md | [ DRAFT ] | President, cabinet, cleaner emergency powers, pardons restricted, succession and incapacity rules, transition rules, public recall referendum, and presidential accountability without categorical official act immunity; war powers moved to Article XVI | | IV | The Judiciary | articles/IV judiciary.md | [ DRAFT ] | 15 justices, 15 year terms, annual seat expiration schedule, narrower Nominations Commission, elected Chief Justice, lower court term limits, judicial ethics, removal, constitutional review | | V | Civil and Political Rights | articles/V rights.md | [ DRAFT ] | Free expression, religion, privacy, search and seizure, due process, equal rights, arms, rights of the accused, digital rights, rights in emergency, conflicting rights; trimmed to more principle level rules | | VI | Democratic Integrity | articles/VI integrity.md | [ DRAFT ] | Anti subversion, foreign interference in elections, media pluralism and algorithmic transparency; ethics now grouped in Article VIII and campaign finance in Article VII | | VII | Campaign Finance and Political Money | articles/VII campaign finance.md | [ DRAFT ] | Citizens United override for non individual entities, mandatory disclosure/tracing to original human donor, public financing, outside spending caps, coordination rules, contractor and crypto restrictions, and enforcement tiers | | VIII | Government Ethics | articles/VIII government ethics.md | [ DRAFT ] | Corruption/bribery/self dealing, financial disclosure, blind trusts, emoluments, conflict resistant investment rules, financial market integrity, civil service merit protection, whistleblower protection, lobbying registration and disclosure | | IX | Citizenship and National Membership | articles/IX citizenship membership.md | [ DRAFT ] | Equal citizenship, overseas political membership, domicile rules, officeholding equality, and proof/administration of national membership | | X | Federalism and the States | articles/X federalism.md | [ DRAFT ] | Enumerated federal powers, reserved state powers, democratic floor enforcement, preemption rules, D.C. statehood, territories, and local government rights | | XI | Amendments | articles/XI amendments.md | [ DRAFT ] | Two track amendment process, unamendable core, pre clearance, post ratification review | | XII | Constitutional Organs | articles/XII constitutional organs.md | [ DRAFT ] | Electoral Commission + Accountability Commission — independent, not a 4th branch | | XIII | Federal Agencies and the Administrative State | articles/XIII federal agencies.md | [ DRAFT ] | Two tier agency framework, for cause removal, anti abolition, revolving door floor, civil service protections, captured agency doctrine, reduced administrative code detail | | XIV | Taxation and Public Revenue | articles/XIV taxation public revenue.md | [ DRAFT ] | Taxing power, revenue integrity, fair administration, transparency of the effective revenue system, and anti weaponization principles for public revenue | | XV | Budget, Public Credit, and Appropriations | articles/XV budget public credit appropriations.md | [ DRAFT ] | Borrowing, appropriations discipline, debt/default resistance, automatic fiscal continuity, transparency, classified spending oversight, and budget integrity | | XVI | War Powers and National Security | articles/XVI war powers national security.md | [ DRAFT ] | Commander in Chief authority, AUMF requirements, withdrawal order enforcement, cyber warfare, nuclear weapons, PMCs, covert ops, domestic deployment, and unlawful orders | | XVII | Foreign Policy and National Security | articles/XVII foreign policy national security.md | [ DRAFT ] | Economic security countermeasures, foreign investment review, foreign influence disclosure, critical infrastructure protection, foreign intelligence court, bounded gray zone response, alliance reliability, and technology/supply chain security | | XVIII | Social, Economic, and Affirmative Rights | articles/XVIII social economic rights.md | [ DRAFT ] | Environmental rights, healthcare, education, housing, right to work, right to organize, living wage, children's rights, and bounded adequacy review remedies | | XIX | Ratification and Transition | articles/XIX ratification transition.md | [ DRAFT ] | Ratification, continuity of law, first elections, institutional transition, first cycle amendment rules, and anti obstruction transition safeguards | Amendments Reserved for future amendments ratified after initial adoption. None yet. | | Title | File | Status | | | | | | | — | (none yet) | — | — | Design Notes Supporting documents explaining the reasoning behind structural choices. | File | Description | | | | | design notes/rationale.md | Why each major departure from the current US Constitution was made | | design notes/comparison.md | Side by side comparison with current US Constitution | | design notes/scorecard.md | Working scoring rubric and baseline ratings to track improvements over time | | design notes/improvement queue.md | Priority ordered revision queue derived from the scorecard | Core Design Principles These principles guide every drafting decision. When in doubt, refer back here. 1. Majority rule with minority protection — majorities govern, but cannot strip fundamental rights from minorities. 2. No permanent minority veto — structural features must not allow a permanent political minority to block majority governance indefinitely. 3. Resilience against backsliding — the constitution must be hard to dismantle from within by bad faith actors. 4. Adaptability — legitimate change must be possible without requiring near unanimity. 5. Accountability — power must be traceable, visible, and removable. 6. Long term thinking — governance structures must be capable of addressing multi generational problems. Drafting Log | Date | Change | | | | | 2026 03 28 | Gap fix pass: resolved several live structural and consistency issues. Article III and Article IX now align on presidential candidate eligibility by giving the Electoral Commission an initial ballot qualification role subject to expedited judicial review. Article X no longer allows sunset fallback to defeat independent agency protections. Article VIII now states the superseded provisions transition rule instead of leaving it open. Article IX vacancy backstops now apply to both Constitutional Organs without converting temporary judicial appointments into permanent unilateral ones. Article II's stale ethics cross reference was corrected, Article XV numbering was cleaned up, and drafting residue notes were removed from moved sections. | | 2026 03 28 | Revision sprint: Article III simplified. Emergency powers, executive orders, pardons, incapacity, succession limits, transition rules, recall, and presidential accountability now use cleaner constitutional triggers with less procedural clutter. The core anti abuse structure remains, but the article reads more like constitutional doctrine than operational code. | | 2026 03 28 | Revision sprint: Article XI compressed and Article XV simplified. Article XI now keeps the same anti abuse architecture with cleaner language around reporting, withdrawal, AUMFs, nuclear first use, covert operations, and domestic deployment. Article XV now preserves the same anti corruption structure while moving many investment, market regulation, and revolving door mechanics to a more constitutional level of abstraction. | | 2026 03 28 | Revision sprint: Article VII democratic floor enforcement simplified and Article XI / XIV boundary sharpened. Article VII now uses a cleaner sequence of Supreme Court finding, congressional remedy, and representation suspension without brittle metrics or automatic special master machinery; a stale education cross reference now correctly points to Article XIII. Article XI now reads more clearly as the armed force article, while Article XIV now expressly excludes sustained hostilities and other uses of military force from gray zone authority. | | 2026 03 28 | Revision sprint: cross reference audit plus simplification of Article V and Article X. Article V now states narrower and more durable rights rules in several areas, including speech exceptions, medical autonomy, digital search, bail, anti discrimination coverage, biometric surveillance, and internet access. Article X now keeps the constitutional architecture while stripping out enforcement metrics, automatic restoration mechanics, and other statutory level detail from agency removal, defunding, inspector general protection, revolving door, sunset, and political appointment provisions. | | 2026 03 28 | Revision sprint: Article IV simplified and Article XIII remedies narrowed. Article IV now uses a fixed annual seat expiration model rather than layered appointment caps; early departures are covered temporarily by Senior Justices until the scheduled appointment date. The Chief Justice is now elected by the active justices, the Judicial Nominations Commission is reduced and narrowed to certification/backstop functions, lower court appointments no longer require vacancy specific shortlists, and the stale 18 year transition reference was corrected to 15 years. Article XIII now states a clearer adequacy review model: courts may declare constitutional floors unmet, require a lawful remedial plan, and police bad faith noncompliance, but may not design programs, choose among materially sufficient policy options, or order taxes or new appropriations as primary remedies. | | 2026 03 28 | Article I Section 8 (Redistricting) strengthened. Partisan symmetry added as explicit criterion (8.2(f)) — maps must not produce systematic partisan advantage disproportionate to statewide vote share; measured by efficiency gap and mean median difference; Commission sets thresholds. Deadlock resolution added (8.6) — if Commission cannot agree, competing proposals certified to D.C. Circuit Chief Judge; 3 judge panel selected at random from other circuits selects among submitted proposals only; no new map drawing. Court remedial order deadline added to 8.5. | 2026 03 27 | Initial scaffold created. Structure and principles defined. | | 2026 03 28 | Article III: Three foreign ties provisions added. Section 2.2A: sole citizenship required — any other citizenship must be renounced before taking office; acquiring foreign citizenship during term = immediate disqualification. Section 2.2B: prior foreign government/military/intelligence service within 20 years is disqualifying; private employment and academic exchange are not. Section 2.2C: enhanced foreign ties disclosure required at ballot qualification — covers prior citizenship, foreign government income, foreign business interests, family members in foreign government; Electoral Commission verifies against intelligence records; false disclosure = disqualification + criminal prosecution. Applies equally to VP. | 2026 03 28 | Article V Section 3 (Religion) expanded. Government neutrality (3.3): must treat all religions and non religion equally; no endorsement, official symbols, or religious proclamations. Public schools (3.4): no official led prayer or religious instruction; students retain private religious expression rights. Religious exemptions cannot harm third parties (3.5): exemptions valid only where burden falls on the claimant, not others; cannot be used to deny legally entitled services or discriminate in public accommodations. Secular basis required (3.6): laws with no cognizable secular justification are invalid; religious support does not invalidate a law, but religious justification alone cannot sustain one. Conscientious objection (3.7): sincere religious or moral conviction against armed combat; moral objections treated equally to religious; alternative service required. | 2026 03 28 | Article IV: Mandatory jurisdiction added (Section 9.2A) — circuit splits on constitutional questions and cases involving federal officials must be accepted. Decision time limits (Section 10) — Supreme Court 12 months, Circuit 6 months, District 90 days; public notice required at 6 months for SCOTUS. Flat pool confirmed. No age requirement (15 year experience floor already in Section 4.5). Closed all open questions. | 2026 03 28 | Article V: Death penalty left to Congress (Section 9.4A) — if authorized, requires unanimous jury, mandatory appeal, bias free administration. Internet access right added (Section 10.6). Minimum wage updated (Section 13.4) — living wage standard constitutionalized, mandatory 5 year review, auto CPI if Congress fails to act. Children's rights added (Section 15) — education, protection from abuse/labor exploitation, right to be heard, best interests standard. Conflicting rights renumbered to Section 16. Closed all open questions. | 2026 03 28 | Article II: Felony conviction two category system (Section 5.5) — corruption/abuse = automatic removal; all others = 2/3 chamber vote with appeal stay; reinstatement path if conviction overturned. Single subject pre enactment challenge (Section 10.7A) — any member may file with Supreme Court; ruled within 15 days; presidential clock tolled. Minimum 120 day session floor (Section 15). Closed all Article II open questions. | 2026 03 28 | Article III: VP terms count toward presidential two term limit (Section 2.4A) — full VP terms count 1:1; prevents VP as workaround. Closed all Article III open questions. | 2026 03 28 | Article I: Added Section 13 (Recall of Elected Federal Officials). Applies to House and Regional Assembly members only (not judges or appointees). Triggered by 20% of votes cast signatures within 120 days. Timing restrictions: not in first 12 months or last 6 months of term; one attempt per term. Removal requires majority + 30% turnout. Vacancy filled by special election within 90 days. Protected conduct clause: recall cannot be based solely on legislative votes. Cross referenced from Article II Section 5.3A. Voting age kept at 18 (closed open question). | 2026 03 28 | Article I: Added Section 12 (Overseas Citizens) — tiered system: under 10 years abroad votes in last state for all federal elections; 10+ years votes federal only in last state; never domiciled citizens assigned to parent's state or federal overseas pool. Updated Section 4.1: RCV ranking floor = number of seats in district, ceiling set by Electoral Commission by regulation. Closed two open questions. | | 2026 03 28 | Decided against full 4th branch. Added Article IX: two Constitutional Organs (Electoral Commission + Accountability Commission). Updated Article VI to reference Article IX for enforcement. Added philosophical rationale to design notes. | | 2026 03 28 | Drafted Article VIII (Amendments) in full. Hybrid unamendable core (explicit list + principled backstop). Pre clearance and post ratification review mechanisms written in. 10 year ratification cooling off period added. | | 2026 03 28 | Drafted Articles I and II in full. STV multi member districts, automatic voter registration, citizens' initiative, 8 geographic regions for Regional Assembly, asymmetric chamber powers, 90 day deadlock rule, automatic continuing resolution to prevent government shutdowns. | | 2026 03 28 | Expanded Article II Section 12 (Budget). Abolished debt ceiling. Prohibited off budget spending. Mandatory public publication within 5 days (machine readable, searchable). 10 year and 30 year fiscal projections required. Annual performance reporting per program. Added Section 12A (Classified Spending): full congressional oversight mandatory, independent audit with clearances, public aggregate disclosure, domestic political use banned, 5 year sunset/reauthorization, mandatory declassification review, whistleblower protection. | | 2026 03 28 | Drafted Preamble. Five sentences. Acknowledges broken founding promise. States the central tension (strong enough to protect, limited enough to remain free). Commits to future generations. Names democratic resilience as a founding obligation. | | 2026 03 28 | Drafted Article III (Executive) in full. Natural born citizenship requirement abolished. Non consecutive term limits. Emergency powers capped at 180 days. Acting official loophole closed (45 day limit). Pardon restrictions. War powers framework. Full incapacity process. Succession line constitutionalized. Transition rules with anti subversion tie in. Public recall referendum added (Section 14). | | 2026 03 28 | Drafted Article IV (Judiciary) in full. 15 justices, 15 year terms, 1 appointment/year. Judicial Nominations Commission depoliticizes vetting. Lower courts get 14/12 year terms. Judicial ethics board in Accountability Commission. Congress cannot strip constitutional review jurisdiction. 10 year time limit on statutory challenges. Transition provisions for existing justices. | | 2026 03 28 | Drafted Article V (Rights and Freedoms) in full. 15 sections covering classic liberties, privacy, digital rights, environmental rights, affirmative rights, economic rights. Absolute rights that can never be suspended. Affirmative rights enforcement mechanism (courts order Congress to act, cannot prescribe policy). Conflicting rights framework. Hate speech provisions added (2.1e, 2.1f, 2.2) — targeted harassment and official incitement only, with explicit narrow construction rules. | | 2026 03 28 | Drafted Article VI (Integrity) in full. Precise anti subversion definition (force/fraud/official coercion only; legal challenges protected). Campaign finance — Citizens United overturned by design. Civil service merit protection constitutionalized. Foreign interference and lobbying disclosure. Media ownership cap (20%). Sitting president not immune from investigation. | | 2026 03 28 | Strengthened Article VI Section 2 (dark money). Added: mandatory tracing to original human donor through all intermediaries; 48 hour real time disclosure above $10k; non profit spending triggers full donor disclosure; LLC/partnership political spending banned without full member disclosure; coordination defined broadly (shared staff, data, consultants = coordinated); foreign money via domestic intermediary explicitly covered. | | 2026 03 28 | Added Article VI Sections 3.8–3.13 (investment restrictions). Officials and covered family members may only hold broad index funds, government bonds, primary residence, pre existing disclosed business interests. Individual stocks, options, sector ETFs, private company investments all prohibited. Forced divestiture tax deferred. 48 hour trade disclosure. Disgorgement + 3× fine + criminal prosecution + removal for violations. Covers Congress, President, judges, senior staff, and their families. | | 2026 03 28 | Added Article VI Section 3A (Financial Market Integrity — SEC teeth). Independent funding via fees not appropriations. Commissioners removable for cause only. Penalty floor: 3× profits + no dollar cap. No neither admit nor deny for major fraud. Criminal referral to Accountability Commission mandatory within 30 days; response required within 180 days. "Too big to prosecute" explicitly prohibited. Emergency asset freeze (72 hour judicial ruling). 7 year executive clawback. Whistleblower rewards 10–30%. 5 year revolving door for senior regulators. Jurisdiction over all new financial instruments. Real time market surveillance. | | 2026 03 28 | Expanded Article VI Section 3A.10 (Revolving Door). Both directions addressed: industry→regulator (2 year recusal, deferred comp forfeiture, golden parachute ban) and regulator→industry (tiered cooling off periods by seniority: 1/3/5 years). Transition stipend constitutionally protected: cannot be eliminated, floor 40% of final salary. Vesting based on years of service (nothing under 2 years, scaled to 60%+ at 10+ years).
